| Financial Stability Board | 2025-10-01 | AI monitoring indicators | Proposes indicators for criticality, concentration, and substitutability of AI third party providers. |
| International Monetary Fund | 2025-04-22 | Global financial stability | Notes elevated valuations and vulnerability to further valuation adjustments under adverse scenarios. |
| arXiv | 2025-10-30 | Inference economics | Provides a framework for cost effectiveness and diminishing returns in LLM inference deployment. |
| arXiv | 2025-12-24 | Productivity scaling evidence | Shows heterogeneous productivity gains across task types, informing monetization and diminishing returns risk. |


![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Source: Financial Stability Board publications and IMF GFSR pages, plus cited 2025 arXiv papers[13](https://www.fsb.org/2024/11/the-financial-stability-implications-of-artificial-intelligence/) .

## 6. Economic Impact Projections of an AI Bubble Burst

An AI bubble burst would most plausibly transmit to the real economy through a macro financial channel rather than through immediate destruction of productive capacity. The key mechanism is a rapid repricing of high duration growth assets, followed by tighter financial conditions, reduced venture and corporate funding, and a pullback in capital expenditure tied to data centers, semiconductors, and cloud infrastructure. The macro impact would likely be nonlinear because AI investment is increasingly concentrated in a small set of large firms and financing channels, and because AI related capital spending is intertwined with energy, construction, and global supply chains.

Across advanced economies, the most material near term downside risks are a negative wealth effect from equity drawdowns, a tightening of credit conditions for high growth firms, and a capex cliff in AI infrastructure buildouts. For emerging markets, the main risks are spillovers through global risk appetite, portfolio outflows, and trade exposure to electronics and capital goods. The medium term outcome depends on whether the correction is a valuation reset with continued diffusion of useful AI, or a broader credit event that forces widespread deleveraging.

This section frames projections using downside risk narratives and macro financial vulnerabilities highlighted by the OECD and IMF, then maps spillovers to adjacent sectors and labor markets.

### 6.1. Macroeconomic scenarios: OECD and IMF downside risk forecasts

OECD and IMF baseline projections published in 2025 point to a slowing but still positive global growth path into 2026, with downside risks explicitly linked to tighter financial conditions, elevated policy uncertainty, and the possibility of financial market corrections. While neither institution publishes an AI bubble specific scenario, their risk framing can be operationalized for an AI led correction by treating it as a large equity repricing that tightens financial conditions, reduces risk appetite, and transmits to the real economy through weaker investment, hiring, and confidence.

Title: Selected OECD and IMF baseline real GDP growth projections

| Institution and publication date | Geography | 2025 real GDP growth (%, y y) | 2026 real GDP growth (%, y y) |
|:---|:---|---:|---:|
| OECD Economic Outlook press release 2025 06 03 | World | 2.9% | 2.9% |
| OECD Economic Outlook press release 2025 06 03 | United States | 1.6% | 1.5% |
| OECD Economic Outlook press release 2025 06 03 | Euro area | 1.0% | 1.2% |
| OECD Economic Outlook press release 2025 06 03 | China | 4.7% | 4.3% |
| IMF World Economic Outlook 2025 10 14 | World | 3.2% | 3.1% |


![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Source: OECD Economic Outlook press release 2025 06 03[14](https://read.oecd-ilibrary.org/en/about/news/press-releases/2025/06/global-economic-outlook-shifts-as-trade-policy-uncertainty-weakens-growth.html) . IMF World Economic Outlook 2025 10 14[26](https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/WEO/Issues/2025/10/14/world-economic-outlook-october-2025) .

How an AI bubble burst maps into OECD and IMF downside risk language.

1) Financial conditions tightening and investment shock.
   - Valuation correction as the initiating shock.
     - The IMF identifies high valuations in some key markets as a forward looking vulnerability and notes that further valuation declines could tighten global financial conditions if the outlook deteriorates.
     - This maps directly to an AI led repricing in which the initial impulse is a sharp decline in equity valuations for AI exposed firms, followed by higher risk premia more broadly.
     - Transmission channels to the real economy include a negative wealth effect, higher cost of equity, wider credit spreads, and tighter lending standards.
     - The IMF also highlights risks of market turmoil and challenges to debt sustainability for highly indebted sovereigns, which is relevant if a repricing triggers broader risk off dynamics and higher term premia.
     - Source basis: IMF GFSR April 2025 vulnerability framing around valuations, market turmoil, and debt sustainability.
     - Source: IMF Global Financial Stability Report April 2025[24](https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/GFSR/Issues/2025/04/22/global-financial-stability-report-april-2025) .
   - Investment and capex sensitivity.
     - OECD baseline narratives emphasize that tighter financial conditions and diminished confidence can weaken growth.
     - In an AI correction, the most direct real economy channel is an investment shock concentrated in AI infrastructure and adjacent supply chains, including data centers, semiconductors, networking, and power and cooling buildouts.
     - The shock can be modeled as a decline in private fixed investment growth, with spillovers to productivity expectations and to labor demand in tech and construction related segments.
     - Source: OECD press release language on tighter financial conditions, diminishing confidence, and heightened policy uncertainty.
     - Source: OECD Economic Outlook press release 2025 06 03[25](https://www.oecd.org/en/about/news/press-releases/2025/06/global-economic-outlook-shifts-as-trade-policy-uncertainty-weakens-growth.html) .

2) Confidence and policy uncertainty amplification.
   - Uncertainty as a multiplier rather than a separate shock.
     - OECD explicitly links weaker growth prospects to elevated policy uncertainty and weaker business and consumer confidence.
     - IMF WEO similarly emphasizes that risks are tilted to the downside and highlights uncertainty and potential financial market corrections as key downside elements.
     - For scenario design, this supports treating an AI repricing as more damaging when it coincides with high policy uncertainty, because firms delay investment and hiring, households raise precautionary savings, and financial intermediaries become more risk averse.
     - Source: OECD press release and OECD Economic Outlook introduction language on uncertainty and confidence[25](https://www.oecd.org/en/about/news/press-releases/2025/06/global-economic-outlook-shifts-as-trade-policy-uncertainty-weakens-growth.html) . Source: IMF WEO October 2025 downside risk framing[26](https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/WEO/Issues/2025/10/14/world-economic-outlook-october-2025) .
   - United States specific risk narrative as a template.
     - OECD notes elevated uncertainty and volatility in equity and bond markets in its United States outlook narrative, consistent with a setting in which a valuation shock can propagate through broader financial conditions.
     - This provides a narrative anchor for a US led AI equity drawdown that transmits globally through financial markets and confidence.
     - Source: OECD United States country note[20](https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/2025/06/oecd-economic-outlook-volume-2025-issue-1_1fd979a8/full-report/united-states_c69a8d2f.html) .

3) Scenario sketch for report use.

   3.1) Mild correction scenario.
   - Shock description.
     - Equity repricing concentrated in AI and mega cap technology.
     - Private market valuations reset, with a pullback in late stage funding and a higher required return for growth assets.
   - Macro transmission.
     - Financial conditions tighten modestly.
     - Real economy impact concentrated in a capex pause and slower hiring in tech and AI adjacent sectors.
   - Consistency with OECD and IMF framing.
     - Aligns with IMF valuation vulnerability language without requiring systemic stress.
     - Aligns with OECD baseline emphasis on confidence and uncertainty as growth drags.
     - Source: IMF GFSR April 2025[24](https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/GFSR/Issues/2025/04/22/global-financial-stability-report-april-2025) . Source: OECD press release 2025 06 03[25](https://www.oecd.org/en/about/news/press-releases/2025/06/global-economic-outlook-shifts-as-trade-policy-uncertainty-weakens-growth.html) .

   3.2) Adverse macro financial scenario.
   - Shock description.
     - Equity drawdown broadens into credit markets.
     - Credit spreads widen for lower rated and cash burn firms, and refinancing risk rises.
   - Macro transmission.
     - Corporate investment contracts beyond tech.
     - Unemployment rises as hiring freezes spread and layoffs extend into cyclical sectors.
   - Financial system propagation.
     - IMF highlights that highly leveraged financial institutions and nonbank financial intermediaries can come under strain in volatile markets, including through deleveraging pressures when facing margin calls and redemptions.
     - This supports modeling a non linear tightening in financial conditions once volatility rises and leverage constraints bind.
     - Source: IMF GFSR April 2025[27](https://meetings.imf.org/en/IMF/Home/Publications/GFSR/Issues/2025/04/22/global-financial-stability-report-april-2025) .

   3.3) Disorderly correction scenario.
   - Shock description.
     - A rapid capex cliff in data centers and semiconductors coincides with a broader risk off episode.
     - Cross border portfolio flows reverse, with emerging markets facing currency pressure and higher external financing costs.
   - Macro transmission.
     - Global investment and trade weaken materially.
     - Sovereign risk premia rise in highly indebted jurisdictions, tightening fiscal constraints.
   - Consistency with IMF framing.
     - IMF explicitly flags risks of market turmoil and challenges to debt sustainability for highly indebted sovereigns, and notes that tighter global financial conditions can significantly impact emerging markets currencies, asset prices, and capital flows.
     - Source: IMF GFSR April 2025[24](https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/GFSR/Issues/2025/04/22/global-financial-stability-report-april-2025) .

Practical implication for policymakers and investors.

- Use OECD and IMF baselines as the reference path, then layer a financial conditions shock and an investment shock concentrated in AI infrastructure and high growth firms.
- Treat uncertainty as an amplifier.
  - When policy uncertainty is elevated, calibrate larger and more persistent investment and hiring responses for a given financial shock, consistent with OECD and IMF downside risk narratives.
  - Source: OECD press release 2025 06 03[25](https://www.oecd.org/en/about/news/press-releases/2025/06/global-economic-outlook-shifts-as-trade-policy-uncertainty-weakens-growth.html) . Source: IMF WEO October 2025[26](https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/WEO/Issues/2025/10/14/world-economic-outlook-october-2025) .
- Monitor whether the correction remains an equity valuation event or becomes a broader credit event.
  - The macro severity hinge is whether stress propagates through leveraged institutions and nonbank financial intermediaries, forcing deleveraging and tightening credit supply, consistent with IMF macro financial vulnerability framing.
  - Source: IMF GFSR April 2025[27](https://meetings.imf.org/en/IMF/Home/Publications/GFSR/Issues/2025/04/22/global-financial-stability-report-april-2025) .

### 6.2. Spillover effects on adjacent tech sectors and global markets

An AI bubble burst would likely propagate through three spillover layers, with feedback loops that can turn an initially sector specific repricing into a broader macro financial shock.

1) Adjacent technology sectors with direct revenue dependence.
   - Semiconductors and semiconductor equipment.
     - A pullback in hyperscaler and frontier model developer capital expenditure would reduce near term demand for accelerators, high bandwidth memory, networking, and advanced packaging.
     - Second order effects typically include a sharper correction in wafer starts, foundry utilization, and equipment order books, amplifying cyclicality through inventory and capacity adjustment dynamics.
     - Spillovers can extend to upstream materials and specialty chemicals, and to downstream original equipment manufacturers that depend on high end compute availability.
   - Cloud and data center supply chain.
     - If expected utilization, pricing, or customer pre commitments reset, projects can be delayed, resized, or cancelled across construction, electrical equipment, cooling, and grid interconnection.
     - The most exposed segments are those with long lead times and high fixed cost commitments, including power delivery equipment, site development, and network buildouts.
     - A correction can also reduce demand for colocation expansions and for premium interconnect services that are justified by aggressive AI workload growth assumptions.
   - Enterprise software, IT services, and cybersecurity.
     - A broad risk off episode can tighten IT budgets and slow discretionary software spend, consulting, and systems integration, even if security spend remains comparatively resilient.
     - The highest sensitivity is typically in new seat growth, expansion deals, and multi year transformation programs, while renewals and compliance driven security controls tend to be stickier.
     - A secondary channel is slower AI product monetization, which can pressure revenue expectations for software vendors that have priced in rapid AI attach rates.

2) Financial market spillovers.
   - Equity market concentration channel.
     - If AI exposed mega cap firms represent a large share of index capitalization, a repricing can transmit quickly to passive portfolios, household wealth, and corporate confidence, raising the probability of a broader consumption and investment slowdown.
     - Concentration also increases the likelihood that volatility in a small set of names drives index level risk metrics, tightening financial conditions even for firms with limited AI exposure.
   - Venture, growth equity, and private credit channel.
     - A valuation reset can reduce exit opportunities, extend holding periods, and increase refinancing risk for venture backed firms, especially those with high cash burn and near term maturities.
     - Down rounds and delayed liquidity events can propagate to employee consumption via reduced equity compensation value and to startup ecosystems via reduced follow on funding.
   - Nonbank leverage and market liquidity channel.
     - The IMF highlights vulnerabilities from high valuations in some key markets, leverage in some financial institutions and their nexus with banking systems, and risks of market turmoil, which is the pathway through which a tech led drawdown can become systemic[24](https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/GFSR/Issues/2025/04/22/global-financial-stability-report-april-2025) .
     - In practice, the transmission risk is highest when crowded positioning, margin financing, and liquidity mismatches coincide with a volatility shock.

3) Global trade and cross border spillovers.
   - Electronics and capital goods trade.
     - Economies integrated into semiconductor and electronics supply chains would face export volatility if AI hardware demand falls, with knock on effects to industrial production, logistics, and related services.
     - The impact is typically non linear because supply chains are capacity constrained during booms and then adjust abruptly when orders are cancelled.
   - Risk appetite and capital flows.
     - Emerging markets can experience portfolio outflows during global risk off episodes, even when the initial shock is sector specific, tightening domestic financial conditions and weakening currencies.
     - The spillover is larger when global investors treat the episode as a signal of broader growth disappointment rather than a contained sector correction.
   - Policy uncertainty interaction.
     - OECD analysis emphasizes that heightened policy uncertainty and tighter financial conditions weigh on growth and investment, implying that an AI correction occurring during elevated uncertainty can magnify spillovers across regions[25](https://www.oecd.org/en/about/news/press-releases/2025/06/global-economic-outlook-shifts-as-trade-policy-uncertainty-weakens-growth.html) .
     - World Bank analysis similarly highlights trade tensions and policy uncertainty as a drag on global growth, which can increase sensitivity to financial shocks[20](https://www.worldbank.org/en/news/press-release/2025/06/10/global-economic-prospects-june-2025-press-release) .

Key sector specific consequences to highlight in contingency planning.

- Semiconductor capital expenditure whiplash.
  - Over ordering during the boom can be followed by inventory corrections and delayed fab and equipment spending, with a lagged impact on suppliers due to backlog unwind.
  - Risk is elevated where capacity additions were justified by a narrow set of AI demand assumptions rather than diversified end markets.
- Data center real assets repricing.
  - If utilization assumptions reset, data center related real estate and infrastructure financing can reprice, affecting lenders, project finance, and the availability of construction debt.
  - The most exposed projects are those with limited pre leasing, weaker counterparties, or power procurement terms that assume sustained high load factors.
- Energy, grid, and water planning timing risk.
  - AI driven load growth expectations influence grid planning and generation investment. A sudden demand reassessment can create stranded or underutilized assets in some regions, while other regions still face structural electrification demand.
  - The IEA identifies data centres as a major driver of stronger US electricity demand growth, and estimates US data centre electricity consumption at around 180.00 TWh in 2024[21](https://www.iea.org/reports/electricity-mid-year-update-2025/demand-global-electricity-use-to-grow-strongly-in-2025-and-2026) .
  - The IEA also expects US electricity demand to grow at about 2.00% per year on average in 2025 to 2027, citing data centre growth as a key reason for upward revisions[22](https://www.iea.org/reports/electricity-2025/demand) .

Title: Selected macro and energy indicators relevant to AI spillovers

| Indicator | Geography | Period | Value (units) |
|:---|:---|:---|---:|
| Global GDP growth forecast | Global | 2026 | 2.9% |
| US GDP growth forecast | United States | 2026 | 1.5% |
| US data centre electricity consumption | United States | 2024 | 180.00 TWh |
| US electricity demand growth forecast | United States | 2026 | 2.2% |


![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Source: OECD Interim Economic Outlook press release dated 23 September 2025, IEA Electricity Mid Year Update 2025, and IEA Electricity 2025 report[23](https://www.oecd.org/en/about/news/press-releases/2025/09/global-economic-outlook-weakens-as-policy-uncertainty-weighs-on-demand.html) .

Investor relevant indicators of spillover severity.

- Breadth of repricing beyond AI equities into credit spreads, funding markets, and bank lending standards.
- Capital expenditure guidance changes from hyperscalers and major semiconductor suppliers, including signals on order cancellations, delivery deferrals, and networking buildout pacing.
- Venture funding volumes, down round frequency, and time to exit as proxies for private market stress and refinancing risk.
- Nonbank stress signals, including margin requirements, prime brokerage financing terms, and liquidity conditions in equity and credit markets.
- Cross border portfolio flow volatility and currency pressure in electronics export oriented emerging markets.
- Data centre pipeline indicators, including pre lease rates, power procurement terms, and interconnection queue dynamics, as early warnings of real asset repricing.

Scenario framing considerations.

- Contained correction scenario.
  - Equity repricing is concentrated in AI exposed names, capex slows but does not collapse, and credit markets remain orderly.
- Cyclical downturn scenario.
  - Capex cuts propagate through semiconductors, equipment, and construction, with broader earnings downgrades and tighter lending standards.
- Systemic stress scenario.
  - A concentrated equity drawdown interacts with leverage and liquidity mismatches, producing market turmoil consistent with the IMF identified vulnerability channels, and tightening financial conditions across sectors and regions[24](https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/GFSR/Issues/2025/04/22/global-financial-stability-report-april-2025) .

### 6.3. Labor market disruption and productivity realization gaps

A bubble burst would not eliminate the underlying technical capability of AI, but it would widen the gap between expected and realized productivity by slowing diffusion, reducing complementary investment, and increasing organizational risk aversion. The labor market impact is therefore likely to combine a cyclical shock in tech and AI adjacent employment with a structural reallocation challenge in which demand shifts toward fewer but more specialized roles, while entry level pathways weaken.

Labor market disruption channels.

- Hiring freezes and layoffs in AI adjacent roles.
  - If venture funding and corporate capital expenditure contract, firms typically reduce hiring in software engineering, data, product management, sales, marketing, and customer success roles tied to AI commercialization.
  - A correction can also reduce demand for contractors and professional services that support AI deployment, including data labeling, model evaluation, compliance consulting, and systems integration.
- Wage polarization and bargaining power shifts.
  - High skill roles that are tightly coupled to scarce capabilities, such as model engineering, distributed systems, security engineering, and AI infrastructure operations, may remain structurally scarce.
  - However, a correction can temporarily compress wage premia in the most overheated segments by increasing the supply of experienced candidates through layoffs and by reducing the number of funded teams competing for talent.
- Entry level pathway risk.
  - Public statements by IMF leadership at the World Economic Forum in Davos in January 2026 emphasized that AI will affect a large share of jobs in advanced economies and that tasks eliminated are often those performed in entry level roles, raising the risk that younger workers face weaker hiring pipelines during a downturn[28](https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2026/jan/23/ai-tsunami-labour-market-youth-employment-says-head-of-imf-davos) .
  - If firms respond to uncertainty by prioritizing senior hires who can deliver immediate output, the result can be a longer term skills pipeline problem, with fewer opportunities for early career workers to accumulate the tacit knowledge needed for mid career productivity.
- Within firm reallocation and task redesign.
  - Even without net job losses, firms may reallocate headcount away from experimentation and toward governance, security, and cost control functions.
  - Roles that combine domain expertise with AI workflow design, such as clinical operations plus AI tooling, legal operations plus AI review systems, or finance plus automation controls, are more likely to expand than generalist roles.

Productivity realization gaps.

- Complementary investment shortfall.
  - Productivity gains from AI require complementary investments in data governance, process redesign, cybersecurity, and workforce training.
  - A bubble burst can cut these budgets early, delaying measurable productivity and increasing the probability that pilots fail to scale.
- Adoption versus frontier capability divergence.
  - Even if frontier models continue to improve, firms may slow deployment due to cost scrutiny, procurement friction, and heightened risk controls.
  - This can widen the gap between technical progress and aggregate productivity, especially when organizations shift from growth objectives to margin protection.
- Cross country dispersion and diffusion sensitivity.
  - OECD analysis highlights that estimated AI contributions to labor productivity growth vary widely across studies and depend on adoption and diffusion.
  - The OECD reports a broad range of predicted annual labor productivity gains from AI of 0.5 to 3.5 percentage points over a ten year horizon across studies, and OECD modelling suggests a G7 range of 0.2 to 1.3 percentage points, implying that a financing shock that slows adoption can materially reduce realized gains even if the technology remains available[25](https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/oecd-compendium-of-productivity-indicators-2025_b024d9e1-en/full-report/productivity-in-a-shifting-geopolitical-and-economic-landscape_7b0e7702.html) .
- Macro productivity context and the risk of disappointment.
  - OECD reporting indicates that productivity growth remained subdued in 2023 and 2024 and that AI impacts are not yet evident in productivity statistics, increasing the risk that a correction reinforces skepticism about near term payoffs and further slows diffusion[26](https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/oecd-compendium-of-productivity-indicators-2025_b024d9e1-en/full-report/executive-summary_541b47db.html) .

Title: Selected ILO labor market indicators relevant to AI adjustment risk

| Indicator | Value (Units) | Year | Notes |
|:---|---:|---:|:---|
| ILO projected global jobs created | 53,000,000.00 Jobs | 2025 | Revised down from 60,000,000.00 Jobs. |
| ILO implied reduction versus prior projection | 7,000,000.00 Jobs | 2025 | Equivalent to the revision magnitude. |
| Global employment growth rate | 1.500% | 2025 | Revised down from 1.700%. |
| Labor income share of GDP | 52.400% | 2024 | Down from 53.000% in 2014. |


![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Source: International Labour Organization, World Employment and Social Outlook Update press release, May 28, 2025[27](https://www.ilo.org/resource/news/global-employment-forecast-downgraded-7-million-jobs-2025-amid-rising) .

Implications for policymakers and investors.

- Policymakers should treat an AI bubble burst as both a cyclical employment shock in tech clusters and a structural transition challenge.
  - Prioritize rapid reemployment pathways through short cycle training aligned to employer demand, wage insurance pilots, and portable benefits for contract workers.
  - Protect complementary investments that enable diffusion, including digital infrastructure, data interoperability, cybersecurity capacity, and workforce upskilling, to reduce the probability of a prolonged productivity disappointment.
  - Use social dialogue and targeted adjustment support to reduce entry level scarring, given the heightened vulnerability of early career roles highlighted in January 2026 IMF leadership remarks[28](https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2026/jan/23/ai-tsunami-labour-market-youth-employment-says-head-of-imf-davos) .
- Investors should stress test labor and adoption assumptions in AI business cases.
  - Model slower enterprise adoption, longer sales cycles, and higher compliance and security costs during a risk off period.
  - Assume higher retention and retraining costs if firms must redeploy staff rather than expand headcount.
  - Evaluate whether portfolio companies have credible plans for productivity measurement, process redesign, and change management, not only model access and compute.

## 7. Regulatory Environment and Policy Gaps

This section evaluates how the current AI regulatory landscape interacts with financial stability risks that can amplify bubble dynamics. The central finding is that most AI governance regimes are designed to manage safety, rights, and accountability risks at the model and deployment level, while the mechanisms that typically dampen speculative cycles in capital markets remain only partially adapted to AI specific valuation drivers such as compute intensity, concentrated supply chains, and opaque private market pricing. As a result, regulatory fragmentation and uneven enforcement capacity can increase uncertainty premia, encourage regulatory arbitrage, and delay corrective market signals until late in the cycle.

### 7.1. Current global AI regulatory frameworks and oversight mechanisms

Across major economies, AI governance is converging on risk based obligations, transparency requirements, and lifecycle accountability, but with uneven binding force and limited integration with macroprudential oversight.

Key binding and quasi binding frameworks shaping AI governance.
- European Union.
  - The EU AI Act is the most comprehensive cross sector AI law currently in force, with a phased application timeline that matters for market stability because compliance costs and deployment constraints arrive in discrete waves rather than smoothly.
  - The AI Act entered into force on August 1, 2024, with prohibitions and AI literacy obligations applying from February 2, 2025, and obligations for general purpose AI models applying from August 2, 2025, while most remaining rules become applicable from August 2, 2026, and certain high risk AI embedded in regulated products transition later to August 2, 2027. This staging can create predictable repricing catalysts for firms whose revenue plans depend on rapid EU scaling. Sources include the European Commission AI Act timeline materials and policy pages[48](https://ai-act-service-desk.ec.europa.eu/en/ai-act/timeline/timeline-implementation-eu-ai-act) .
  - Oversight architecture includes the European AI Office and Member State authorities, supported by EU level bodies such as the AI Board, Scientific Panel, and Advisory Forum, which improves coordination but also introduces multi layer supervisory complexity that can slow interpretive clarity for investors[49](https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/policies/regulatory-framework-ai) .
- United States.
  - The United States remains primarily standards and sector enforcement driven rather than governed by a single comprehensive AI statute.
  - A key recent change relevant to regulatory certainty is that Executive Order 14110, issued October 30, 2023, was rescinded on January 20, 2025, which increased policy volatility for firms that had built compliance roadmaps around its deadlines[50](https://www.nist.gov/artificial-intelligence/executive-order-safe-secure-and-trustworthy-artificial-intelligence) .
  - NIST AI Risk Management Framework 1.0, published January 26, 2023, remains a widely referenced voluntary baseline for AI risk governance, and NIST also published a Generative AI Profile on July 26, 2024, which many firms use as a de facto control catalog even without formal legal mandate[25](https://www.nist.gov/publications/artificial-intelligence-risk-management-framework-generative-artificial-intelligence) .
  - For investor protection, the SEC has demonstrated willingness to enforce against misleading AI related claims, including a March 18, 2024 action against two investment advisers for false and misleading statements about their use of AI, which is relevant to bubble dynamics because it targets narrative inflation and marketing driven mispricing[51](https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2024-36) .
- United Kingdom.
  - The United Kingdom has emphasized a pro innovation approach using existing regulators and guidance rather than a single horizontal AI law, while also building frontier AI safety capacity through dedicated institutions.
  - The UK is also a signatory to the Council of Europe AI Framework Convention, which can influence domestic governance expectations even before full ratification and implementation[52](https://www.coe.int/en/web/artificial-intelligence/the-framework-convention-on-artificial-intelligence) .
- China.
  - China has implemented targeted rules for generative AI services, including the Interim Measures for the Management of Generative AI Services, effective August 15, 2023, issued by the Cyberspace Administration of China with multiple co issuing regulators, reflecting a multi agency oversight model that can rapidly shape product availability and commercialization pathways[53](https://www.chinalawtranslate.com/en/generative-ai-interim/) .

International and cross border governance instruments.
- Council of Europe Framework Convention on AI and human rights, democracy and the rule of law.
  - Adopted May 17, 2024 and opened for signature September 5, 2024, described by the Council of Europe as the first international legally binding treaty in this field, with technology neutral obligations across the AI lifecycle. This can reduce fragmentation over time but near term adds another compliance layer for multinational firms[52](https://www.coe.int/en/web/artificial-intelligence/the-framework-convention-on-artificial-intelligence) .
- G7 Hiroshima AI Process.
  - The Hiroshima Process International Code of Conduct for organizations developing advanced AI systems is a non binding but influential set of expectations around risk management, transparency, and post deployment monitoring, which can become a soft law benchmark for procurement and due diligence[54](https://g7.utoronto.ca/summit/2023hiroshima/231030-ai-code-of-conduct.html) .
- ISO and management system standards.
  - ISO IEC 42001 is an AI management system standard published in December 2023, providing auditable governance structures that can be used by firms to signal maturity to regulators and investors, but adoption is voluntary and uneven[55](https://www.iso.org/standard/42001) .

Adequacy for managing bubble dynamics.
- Strengths.
  - Risk based compliance regimes and enforcement against misleading claims can reduce narrative driven mispricing and improve baseline governance quality[51](https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2024-36) .
  - Phased implementation timelines, especially in the EU, provide known regulatory milestones that can be incorporated into stress testing and scenario analysis[48](https://ai-act-service-desk.ec.europa.eu/en/ai-act/timeline/timeline-implementation-eu-ai-act) .
- Limitations.
  - Most AI laws focus on safety, rights, and product compliance, not on leverage, liquidity, valuation opacity, or interconnected exposures that drive systemic market instability.
  - Oversight is often split across digital regulators, competition authorities, privacy regulators, and sector supervisors, creating gaps where no single authority owns AI related macro financial risk monitoring.

Title: Selected AI governance instruments and key dates relevant to market repricing risk

| Instrument and jurisdiction | Type | Key date | Key date value |
|:---|:---:|:---|---:|
| EU AI Act | Binding regulation | Entry into force date | 2024-08-01 |
| EU AI Act | Binding regulation | General purpose AI obligations apply | 2025-08-02 |
| EU AI Act | Binding regulation | Most rules applicable and enforcement starts | 2026-08-02 |
| US Executive Order 14110 | Executive action | Rescinded date | 2025-01-20 |
| NIST AI RMF 1.0 | Voluntary standard | Publication date | 2023-01-26 |
| China Interim Measures for Generative AI Services | Binding administrative measures | Effective date | 2023-08-15 |
| Council of Europe AI Framework Convention | International treaty | Opened for signature date | 2024-09-05 |

Source: European Commission AI Act policy and timeline pages [48](https://ai-act-service-desk.ec.europa.eu/en/ai-act/timeline/timeline-implementation-eu-ai-act). NIST Executive Order page and AI RMF publication record [56](https://www.nist.gov/publications/artificial-intelligence-risk-management-framework-ai-rmf-10). China Interim Measures translation and metadata [53](https://www.chinalawtranslate.com/en/generative-ai-interim/). Council of Europe convention page [52](https://www.coe.int/en/web/artificial-intelligence/the-framework-convention-on-artificial-intelligence).

Implications for the AI bubble thesis.
- Regulatory milestones can act as synchronized catalysts for valuation resets, especially where compliance costs are front loaded or where product availability is constrained.
- Voluntary standards improve governance signaling but can also enable selective disclosure, where firms highlight adherence without providing comparable, audited metrics, sustaining valuation dispersion in private markets.

### 7.2. Jurisdictional divergences in AI governance and financial regulation

Divergences across jurisdictions create three market stability risks: regulatory arbitrage, inconsistent disclosure quality, and asynchronous shocks to revenue and cost expectations.

Divergence patterns that matter for global market stability.
- Horizontal AI law versus sectoral approach.
  - The EU AI Act is a horizontal, cross sector regime with explicit obligations for general purpose AI models and high risk systems, while the United States relies more on voluntary standards and sector specific enforcement, and the United Kingdom emphasizes regulator led guidance and principles. This divergence increases compliance complexity for multinational AI firms and can delay global product rollouts, affecting revenue timing assumptions embedded in valuations[56](https://www.nist.gov/publications/artificial-intelligence-risk-management-framework-ai-rmf-10) .
- Binding obligations versus soft law.
  - The G7 Hiroshima Process Code of Conduct is influential but non binding, while the Council of Europe Framework Convention is positioned as legally binding once ratified and implemented, and the EU AI Act is directly binding EU law. This mix can produce uneven compliance investments, where firms optimize for the strictest market while under investing elsewhere, increasing tail risk of enforcement events[52](https://www.coe.int/en/web/artificial-intelligence/the-framework-convention-on-artificial-intelligence) .
- Policy stability and reversals.
  - The rescission of US Executive Order 14110 on January 20, 2025 illustrates that executive driven AI policy can change quickly, which increases discount rates applied to long duration AI business models and can amplify boom bust cycles when expectations reset[50](https://www.nist.gov/artificial-intelligence/executive-order-safe-secure-and-trustworthy-artificial-intelligence) .
- Content and information governance emphasis.
  - China’s generative AI measures emphasize content controls and security aligned obligations for public facing services, which can constrain addressable markets and shape domestic competitive dynamics differently from the EU’s product safety and rights based framing[53](https://www.chinalawtranslate.com/en/generative-ai-interim/) .

Financial regulation alignment gaps.
- Disclosure regimes for AI related claims are not harmonized.
  - The SEC has taken enforcement action against misleading AI claims in investment adviser marketing, but comparable enforcement intensity and definitions of AI related claims vary across jurisdictions, enabling cross border marketing arbitrage[51](https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2024-36) .
- Private market transparency differs sharply.
  - Many jurisdictions have limited standardized disclosure for private AI company valuations, compute commitments, and customer concentration, which can lead to global mispricing when late stage private valuations anchor public comparables.

Practical instability channels created by divergences.
- Compliance cost shocks.
  - EU phased obligations can create step changes in compliance spending and product redesign, while other markets may not impose similar costs at the same time, producing earnings volatility and cross listed valuation dispersion[48](https://ai-act-service-desk.ec.europa.eu/en/ai-act/timeline/timeline-implementation-eu-ai-act) .
- Fragmented incident reporting.
  - Without consistent cross border incident reporting norms, negative events can be disclosed unevenly, delaying price discovery and increasing the probability of abrupt repricing.
- Regulatory arbitrage in model deployment.
  - Firms may prioritize launching higher risk features in jurisdictions with lighter ex ante controls, which can accelerate adoption metrics used in fundraising narratives, while storing latent legal risk that later triggers enforcement and valuation corrections.

Policy coordination opportunities.
- Use the Council of Europe Convention and G7 Hiroshima Process as convergence anchors for baseline lifecycle governance, while separately coordinating financial stability monitoring through securities regulators and central banks.
- Encourage mutual recognition of credible governance certifications such as ISO IEC 42001, paired with minimum disclosure requirements to prevent certification based signaling from becoming a substitute for comparable metrics[55](https://www.iso.org/standard/42001) .

### 7.3. Identified regulatory blind spots in AI investment and market stability

The most consequential blind spots are the ones that let capital form quickly and valuations rise quickly while investors still lack clear, comparable information about unit economics, compute and energy liabilities, and concentration risks. In practice, this means markets can price AI exposure on narratives and momentum rather than on auditable operating facts, increasing the likelihood of mispricing and synchronized repricing.

Blind spots at the intersection of AI governance and capital markets.
- AI specific disclosure gaps in public markets.
  - Existing securities disclosure regimes generally require disclosure of material risks, but there is still no widely adopted, globally standardized baseline for AI specific drivers that can be financially material.
  - Commonly missing or inconsistently reported items include:

    - Training and inference compute commitments and the duration of those commitments.
    - Long term cloud capacity reservations and minimum spend obligations.
    - Model evaluation results, safety testing coverage, and incident reporting metrics.
    - Dependency on a small number of foundation model providers or a single cloud platform.
  - When these items are not disclosed in a consistent way, narrative metrics can dominate.
  - This increases the risk that many issuers are priced using similar stories and assumptions, which can lead to correlated corrections when costs, constraints, or compliance obligations become visible.
- AI washing and marketing claims.
  - Enforcement exists, but it is still uneven relative to the volume of AI related marketing.
  - A clear example is the SEC enforcement action announced on 2024-03-18 against Delphia (USA) Inc. and Global Predictions Inc. for false and misleading AI related claims, with combined civil penalties of 400,000.00 USD.
  - The broader market still lacks consistent definitions and auditability for AI capability claims, especially in private fundraising materials and cross border offerings.
  - The result is an information environment where investors may not be able to distinguish:

    - Demonstrated capabilities from aspirational roadmaps.
    - Production deployments from prototypes.
    - Measured performance from selectively presented benchmarks.
- Private market valuation opacity.
  - Late stage AI rounds often include structured terms that can raise headline valuations without raising the economic value of common equity.
  - These can include liquidation preferences, participation features, ratchets, and secondary transactions.
  - Because these terms are not consistently disclosed, market participants can over infer enterprise value from a single headline number.

Blind spots in systemic risk monitoring.
- Compute and energy as quasi financial exposures.
  - Large scale compute procurement and data center buildouts can create long dated fixed commitments.
  - Economically, these commitments can behave like leverage because they lock in future cash outflows and reduce flexibility during demand shocks.
  - Yet these exposures are not consistently captured in standardized reporting or prudential style stress testing frameworks.
- Concentration and single point of failure risk.
  - AI supply chains remain concentrated across:

    - A small number of hyperscale cloud providers.
    - A small number of advanced chip designers and accelerator platforms.
    - A limited set of advanced semiconductor manufacturing nodes.
  - Financial regulators often monitor concentration through traditional counterparty and vendor risk lenses.
  - That approach can miss AI specific dependency mapping, such as whether many firms rely on the same model provider, the same cloud region, or the same accelerator supply chain.
- Cross border spillovers.
  - Divergent AI rules can shift deployment, product design, and revenue recognition across borders quickly.
  - Macroprudential monitoring frameworks are not yet routinely integrating AI regulatory shocks into financial stability assessments, even though compliance timelines can create predictable repricing catalysts.

Blind spots in enforcement capacity and timing.
- Phased regulation can delay corrective signals.
  - The EU AI Act entered into force on 2024-08-01, but its obligations apply in stages.
  - Key staged applicability dates include:

    - 2025-02-02, when prohibitions and AI literacy obligations begin to apply.
    - 2025-08-02, when governance rules and obligations for general purpose AI models begin to apply.
    - 2026-08-02, when the majority of rules apply and enforcement starts broadly at national and EU level.
  - This staging can allow rapid scaling and valuation expansion before compliance costs, operational constraints, and enforcement risk are fully priced.
- Multi agency fragmentation.
  - Where oversight is split across privacy, consumer protection, competition, and sector regulators, no single body may have the mandate to connect AI deployment risk with market wide leverage and liquidity conditions.
  - This fragmentation can delay detection of system wide build ups, especially when risks are distributed across many firms but share common infrastructure dependencies.

Actionable gap closing measures that directly reduce bubble risk.
- Standardize AI specific issuer disclosures.
  - Require consistent reporting of compute commitments, major supplier concentration, model evaluation coverage, incident metrics, and material regulatory dependencies.
  - Use safe harbor provisions to encourage candid disclosure while still penalizing materially misleading statements.
  - Focus on comparability across issuers so investors can separate growth from hidden cost and constraint.
- Extend anti misleading claim enforcement to issuers and intermediaries.
  - Build on the SEC precedent by clarifying what constitutes materially misleading AI capability claims.
  - Require substantiation for marketing statements in both public and private placements, including clear documentation of what is deployed in production versus planned.
- Integrate AI infrastructure exposures into financial stability monitoring.
  - Encourage central banks and supervisors to treat large compute and data center commitments as stress test variables.
  - Model these exposures similarly to other large, contract driven cost shocks, including scenarios where demand falls, energy prices rise, or capacity becomes constrained.
- Promote auditable governance baselines.
  - Encourage adoption of auditable management system standards such as ISO IEC 42001.
  - Treat certification as a governance baseline, not as a substitute for quantitative disclosure of compute liabilities, concentration, and incident experience.

Title: Enforcement and compliance timing signals that can trigger repricing events

| Category | Example instrument | Date | Value (Units) |
|:---|:---|:---:|---:|
| Investor protection enforcement | SEC AI washing settlement civil penalties total | 2024-03-18 | 400,000.00 USD |
| EU compliance milestone | EU AI Act prohibitions and AI literacy obligations apply | 2025-02-02 | N/A |
| EU compliance milestone | EU AI Act governance rules and GPAI obligations apply | 2025-08-02 | N/A |
| EU compliance milestone | EU AI Act majority of rules apply and broad enforcement starts | 2026-08-02 | N/A |


![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Source: SEC press release on charges against Delphia (USA) Inc. and Global Predictions Inc. and related reporting on penalties[28](https://www.investopedia.com/sec-charges-two-investment-advisers-with-making-false-misleading-ai-claims-8610461) . European Commission AI Act timeline materials[14](https://ai-act-service-desk.ec.europa.eu/en/ai-act/timeline/timeline-implementation-eu-ai-act) .

Implications for investors and policymakers.
- Investors should treat regulatory milestones and enforcement trends as valuation relevant catalysts, not as background legal risk.
- Investors should explicitly test whether an issuer can sustain margins if compute prices, cloud contract minimums, or compliance costs rise faster than revenue.
- Policymakers seeking to mitigate bubble dynamics should prioritize disclosure and stress testing tools that accelerate price discovery, rather than relying only on product compliance rules that may arrive after capital has already been committed.
- Supervisors should prioritize mapping shared AI infrastructure dependencies across firms to identify correlated failure modes that traditional counterparty reporting can miss.

Source notes.
- ISO IEC 42001 is published as ISO IEC 42001:2023 and specifies requirements for an AI management system[29](https://www.iso.org/standard/42001) .

## 8. Investor Risk Assessment Framework

This framework translates the earlier warning indicators and technical reality checks into an investor ready process that can be applied consistently across public equities, private venture, and infrastructure adjacent AI exposures. It is designed to reduce narrative driven decision making by forcing explicit assumptions about unit economics, compute and energy constraints, regulatory friction, and exit conditions, then testing those assumptions under adverse scenarios that reflect the current funding concentration and large ticket deal environment in AI markets.

### 8.1. Due diligence criteria for AI investment evaluation

Core diligence should be structured as an investment memo checklist with explicit pass fail gates, clear evidence requirements, and decision ownership. Each gate should specify.
- What must be true to proceed.
- What evidence is acceptable.
- Who signs off.
- What remediation is required if the gate fails.

Financial health and cash flow durability.
- Revenue quality assessment.
  - Customer concentration by revenue share and gross margin contribution, including top 10 customers and any single customer exceeding a defined threshold.
  - Contract structure and durability, including contract length, renewal rates, churn, expansion, and termination rights.
  - Pricing power under competitive pressure, including discounting trends, renewal uplift, and competitive win loss evidence.
  - Revenue recognition and billing quality, including deferred revenue, unbilled receivables, and any material non standard terms.
- Gross margin decomposition with cost drivers that can be stress tested.
  - Model serving costs separated into compute, storage, networking, and observability.
  - Third party API costs, including minimum commitments, rate limits, and pass through clauses.
  - Cloud commitments and reserved capacity, including take or pay exposure and break clauses.
  - Human in the loop costs, including labeling, review, escalation, and quality assurance.
  - Support and customer success costs where required to maintain retention.
- Cash burn mapping to a runway view that includes committed obligations.
  - Baseline burn and burn under growth plan, including hiring plan, compensation inflation, and sales and marketing ramp.
  - Committed compute spend, reserved instances, and any prepaid credits with expiry.
  - Debt service, covenants, and any contingent liabilities.
  - Working capital sensitivity, including collections, payment terms, and chargebacks.
- Capital intensity classification and scalability.
  - Software like AI applications versus compute intensive model training, inference at scale, and data center adjacent businesses.
  - Unit economics by product line, including gross margin trajectory under volume growth and model improvements.
  - Capex and opex split, including capitalization policies for software and model development.
- Liquidity and refinancing risk review.
  - Dependence on debt funded capex in the broader AI infrastructure cycle.
  - Refinancing windows, covenant headroom, and sensitivity to interest rates and utilization.
  - Counterparty risk for key suppliers, cloud providers, and accelerator vendors.

Technology viability and defensibility.
- Model and product differentiation evidence tied to measurable outcomes.
  - Performance on relevant tasks using reproducible evaluation protocols, including baseline comparisons and ablation results.
  - Production metrics, including latency, uptime, error rates, and cost per successful task.
  - Safety controls in production, including policy enforcement, monitoring, and incident postmortems.
  - Roadmap realism, including what improvements depend on new data, new models, or new hardware.
- Data advantage audit.
  - Data rights and provenance, including licenses, consent, and contractual restrictions.
  - Refresh cadence and data drift monitoring, including how quickly models adapt to new information.
  - Exposure to copyright, privacy, and sector specific data restrictions, including documented legal positions and mitigation plans.
  - Data moat durability, including whether competitors can replicate the dataset through partnerships or public sources.
- Compute strategy review.
  - Dependence on a single cloud or accelerator vendor, including pricing leverage and supply constraints.
  - Portability across clouds and accelerators, including containerization, orchestration, and model runtime compatibility.
  - Inference cost optimization capability, including quantization, batching, caching, distillation, and routing.
  - Capacity planning, including peak load behavior, multi region failover, and cost under burst demand.
- Security and resilience review.
  - Model theft risk, including weight exfiltration controls, access governance, and watermarking strategy where applicable.
  - Prompt injection and data exfiltration defenses, including red teaming results and secure tool use patterns.
  - Incident response maturity, including detection, containment, recovery objectives, and customer notification processes.
  - Supply chain security, including dependency scanning, model artifact integrity, and CI CD controls.
- Regulatory readiness assessment for target markets.
  - Governance, documentation, and monitoring capabilities aligned to emerging AI regulation.
  - Auditability, including model cards, data documentation, and change management.
  - Sector specific compliance where relevant, including healthcare, finance, and critical infrastructure.

Market positioning and competitive dynamics.
- Clear definition of the economic buyer and the budget line item.
  - Evidence that spend is not purely experimental, including renewal behavior, multi year commitments, and expansion.
  - ROI proof points, including time saved, error reduction, revenue uplift, or risk reduction with customer references.
  - Procurement and security gating, including typical blockers and time to close.
- Competitive mapping with explicit substitution pathways.
  - Open source substitution risk, including cost to self host and performance parity thresholds.
  - Platform bundling risk from hyperscalers, including feature overlap, pricing pressure, and distribution leverage.
  - Switching costs and lock in, including integrations, workflow embedding, and data portability.
- Distribution and go to market diligence.
  - Sales cycle length by segment, including pilot to production conversion rates.
  - Channel dependence, including reseller margins, co sell reliance, and partner concentration.
  - Integration requirements, including implementation effort, professional services dependency, and time to value.
- Ecosystem dependency analysis.
  - Upstream inputs such as cloud credits, accelerator availability, and third party model access.
  - Downstream dependencies such as app stores, enterprise platforms, and identity providers.
  - Contractual and technical risks if a platform changes terms, pricing, or access.

Governance and disclosure quality.
- Board oversight of AI risk, cybersecurity, and financial controls.
  - Evidence of regular risk review, including dashboards, incident reporting, and remediation tracking.
  - Independence and expertise, including whether the board has credible AI and security competence.
- Transparency on model limitations, safety incidents, and cost structure.
  - Disclosure discipline, including customer facing documentation and internal incident logs.
  - Consistency between marketing claims and measured performance.
- For private deals, investor rights review.
  - Information rights, pro rata, liquidation preferences, and governance protections.
  - Change of control, related party transactions, and protective provisions.

Practical diligence outputs.
- A unit economics model that ties revenue to compute, energy, and labor inputs.
  - Scenario analysis for inference cost declines, price compression, and utilization changes.
  - Sensitivity to cloud pricing, accelerator availability, and third party API rate changes.
- A technical diligence report that states what must be true for the product to remain competitive over 24 months.
  - Explicit assumptions on model progress, data access, and cost curves.
  - Clear failure modes, including commoditization triggers and platform displacement.
- A regulatory and litigation risk register with quantified downside ranges.
  - Probability weighted scenarios for enforcement, private litigation, and contractual disputes.
  - Mitigations with owners, timelines, and residual risk.

Recent indicators relevant to AI investment concentration and exit conditions.

Title: Global VC and Exit Indicators from KPMG Venture Pulse

| Metric | Value (USD) | Period | Source |
|:---|---:|:---|:---|
| Global VC investment | 368,300,000,000.00 USD | 2024 | KPMG Venture Pulse press release[30](https://kpmg.com/xx/en/media/press-releases/2025/01/2024-global-vc-investment-rises-to-368-billion-dollars.html) . |
| Global VC investment | 120,700,000,000.00 USD | Q3 2025 | KPMG Venture Pulse Q3 2025 global trends page[37](https://kpmg.com/xx/en/our-insights/operations/venture-pulse-q3.html) . |
| Global VC investment | 138,100,000,000.00 USD | Q4 2025 | KPMG Venture Pulse press release[36](https://kpmg.com/xx/en/media/press-releases/2026/01/kpmg-private-enterprise-venture-pulse.html) . |
| Global exit value | 549,200,000,000.00 USD | 2025 | KPMG Venture Pulse press release[36](https://kpmg.com/xx/en/media/press-releases/2026/01/kpmg-private-enterprise-venture-pulse.html) . |


![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Source: KPMG Private Enterprise Venture Pulse press releases and Q3 2025 global trends page[36](https://kpmg.com/xx/en/media/press-releases/2026/01/kpmg-private-enterprise-venture-pulse.html) .

Implications for concentration and exits.
- Concentration risk is structurally elevated when late stage deal values dominate while deal counts remain muted, increasing dependence on a small number of large financings and a small set of perceived category leaders[36](https://kpmg.com/xx/en/media/press-releases/2026/01/kpmg-private-enterprise-venture-pulse.html) .
- Exit conditions improved materially in 2025 versus the prior year, with global exit value reaching 549,200,000,000.00 USD, supporting a more credible liquidity pathway for mature AI companies, while still requiring diligence on timing, IPO readiness, and M and A optionality[36](https://kpmg.com/xx/en/media/press-releases/2026/01/kpmg-private-enterprise-venture-pulse.html) .
- The Q3 2025 to Q4 2025 increase in global VC investment from 120,700,000,000.00 USD to 138,100,000,000.00 USD indicates continued capital availability, but the diligence posture should assume higher selectivity and higher proof thresholds for defensibility and unit economics[37](https://kpmg.com/xx/en/our-insights/operations/venture-pulse-q3.html) .

Pass fail gate examples to operationalize the checklist.
1. Revenue durability gate.
   - Pass if net revenue retention and renewal performance are supported by cohort data and customer references, and pricing holds under competitive bids.
   - Fail if growth is primarily pilot driven, discount driven, or concentrated in a small number of customers without contractual durability.
2. Gross margin viability gate.
   - Pass if gross margin is explainable by controllable drivers and improves with scale through inference optimization and operational leverage.
   - Fail if margins depend on temporary credits, non repeatable pricing, or unbounded human in the loop labor.
3. Compute dependency gate.
   - Pass if the company can operate across at least 2 viable compute supply options or has contractual protections and a credible portability plan.
   - Fail if a single vendor dependency creates existential pricing or supply risk.
4. Data rights gate.
   - Pass if data provenance and rights are documented and defensible for current and planned use.
   - Fail if core training or product data has unclear rights, weak consent, or unresolved legal exposure.
5. Exit readiness gate.
   - Pass if the company has a credible path to an exit window supported by governance maturity, audit readiness, and predictable unit economics.
   - Fail if the business requires sustained subsidized growth without a clear route to profitability or strategic acquisition rationale.

### 8.2. Red flag detection: valuation, financing structure, business model viability

Red flags should be treated as triggers for deeper diligence, valuation haircuts, or deal structure changes.

Valuation and expectations risk.
- Valuation justified primarily by total addressable market narratives without a credible path to gross margin expansion.
- Multiples that implicitly assume rapid commoditization proof is false, for example assuming sustained pricing power while competitors can replicate features using open models.
- Growth that is heavily credit or incentive driven, such as revenue inflated by cloud credits, reseller rebates, or non recurring pilot fees.
- Private rounds with step up valuations despite flat or deteriorating unit economics.

Financing structure and liquidity risk.
- Complex preference stacks that make common equity outcomes fragile, including multiple liquidation preference layers and aggressive participation features.
- Frequent use of structured equity, vendor financing, or off balance sheet commitments that obscure true leverage.
- Debt funded capex exposure without contracted cash flows, especially for compute and data center adjacent plays.
- Reliance on a narrow set of mega rounds or a small number of strategic investors, consistent with the observed concentration of large AI financings in recent VC markets [59](https://kpmg.com/xx/en/media/press-releases/2026/01/kpmg-private-enterprise-venture-pulse.html).

Business model viability and cost realism.
- Inference economics that do not improve with scale because usage is dominated by long context, high token, or low latency workloads.
- Customer value propositions that depend on replacing labor but ignore integration, compliance, and change management costs.
- High churn masked by aggressive new logo acquisition.
- Safety and compliance treated as optional, increasing the probability of deployment delays, fines, or forced product redesign.

Platform and supply chain dependency.
- Single vendor dependence for accelerators, cloud, or distribution, with no credible portability plan.
- Roadmap dependence on future hardware availability or energy access without secured contracts.

Market structure and systemic risk signals.
- Extreme concentration of returns and index exposure in a small set of AI linked mega cap firms, raising correction risk if expectations are missed, consistent with IMF warnings about stretched valuations and market concentration risks [60](https://www.imf.org/external/pubs/ft/ar/2025/in-focus/rising-financial-stability-risks/).

Operational integrity.
- Weak financial controls, unclear revenue recognition, or inconsistent KPI definitions.
- Security incidents or model misuse events without transparent disclosure and remediation.

Actionable investor responses to red flags.
- Require milestone based tranching tied to revenue quality and gross margin targets.
- Reprice using higher discount rates and lower terminal margins.
- Add covenants around compute commitments, debt incurrence, and related party transactions.
- For public equities, cap position sizing and use scenario based drawdown limits tied to concentration risk metrics highlighted by the IMF [43](https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/GFSR/Issues/2025/04/22/global-financial-stability-report-april-2025).

### 8.3. Valuation adjustment and stress-testing methodologies for AI firms

AI investing requires valuation methods that explicitly model compute, energy, and regulatory friction as binding constraints, then stress those constraints under adverse market and funding conditions.

Valuation adjustment methods.
- Unit economics first valuation where gross profit is modeled as revenue minus variable inference costs, data licensing, and human in the loop costs, then scaled with realistic utilization and pricing curves.
- Probability weighted scenario valuation with at least three cases.
  - Base case assumes steady adoption and gradual cost declines.
  - Downside case assumes slower enterprise rollout, higher compliance costs, and pricing compression.
  - Severe case assumes funding shock and rapid multiple compression similar to prior tech de rating episodes.
- Excess return on invested capital lens for capital intensive AI infrastructure and compute adjacent firms, separating construction risk, utilization risk, and power price risk.
- Concentration adjusted discount rates that increase the equity risk premium when revenue depends on a small number of hyperscalers or a small number of customers.

Stress testing design.
- Funding and liquidity shock.
  - Model a sudden reduction in late stage capital availability and a longer time to exit, consistent with the observed reliance on large late stage rounds in recent AI funding cycles [59](https://kpmg.com/xx/en/media/press-releases/2026/01/kpmg-private-enterprise-venture-pulse.html).
  - For private companies, test whether the firm can reach cash flow breakeven before the next financing under reduced growth.
- Cost of compute shock.
  - Increase inference cost per unit and reduce availability, then test gross margin and customer retention.
  - Apply sensitivity to cloud pricing changes and accelerator supply constraints.
- Regulatory and litigation shock.
  - Add compliance cost layers and delay revenue recognition to reflect deployment gating.
  - Apply downside probability to data rights disputes and model output liability.
- Demand shock and pricing compression.
  - Reduce net revenue retention and apply competitive price cuts, especially where differentiation is weak.
- Concentration and correlation shock.
  - For public market portfolios, run factor stress tests where AI linked mega cap equities experience correlated drawdowns, consistent with IMF analysis that high valuations and market concentration can amplify correction risk [43](https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/GFSR/Issues/2025/04/22/global-financial-stability-report-april-2025).

Implementation guidance for investors.
- Require every AI investment to produce a stress tested bridge from revenue to compute and energy inputs, not just a top line forecast.
- Use valuation haircuts when the investment thesis depends on continued availability of mega rounds or unusually favorable exit markets, even though exit activity improved in 2025 [59](https://kpmg.com/xx/en/media/press-releases/2026/01/kpmg-private-enterprise-venture-pulse.html).
- For venture portfolios, track exposure to generative AI deal concentration and stage risk, noting that generative AI VC deal value reached 49,200,000,000.00 USD in the first half of 2025 with larger average deal sizes, which can increase downside convexity if sentiment turns [61](https://www.ey.com/en_ie/newsroom/2025/06/generative-ai-vc-funding-49-2b-h1-2025-ey-report).

Title: Example stress test parameter set for AI firm valuation models

| Shock variable | Base assumption | Stress assumption | Rationale source |
|:---|---:|---:|:---|
| Global VC investment level | 120,700,000,000.00 USD per quarter | 60,350,000,000.00 USD per quarter | Uses Q3 2025 global VC as reference and applies 50.000% funding shock for downside planning [58](https://kpmg.com/xx/en/media/press-releases/2025/10/global-vc-investment-rises-in-q3-25.html). |
| Exit value environment | 549,200,000,000.00 USD per year | 274,600,000,000.00 USD per year | Uses 2025 global exit value as reference and applies 50.000% exit contraction to reflect window closure risk [59](https://kpmg.com/xx/en/media/press-releases/2026/01/kpmg-private-enterprise-venture-pulse.html). |
| Generative AI VC deal value | 49,200,000,000.00 USD per half year | 24,600,000,000.00 USD per half year | Uses H1 2025 generative AI VC deal value as reference and applies 50.000% contraction to test late stage dependency [61](https://www.ey.com/en_ie/newsroom/2025/06/generative-ai-vc-funding-49-2b-h1-2025-ey-report). |


![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Source: KPMG Venture Pulse Q3 2025 and Q4 2025 press releases and EY Generative AI Key Deals and Market Insights summary [61](https://www.ey.com/en_ie/newsroom/2025/06/generative-ai-vc-funding-49-2b-h1-2025-ey-report).

## 9. Sustainable AI Investment Principles

Sustainable AI investing should be treated as a discipline of underwriting long duration technology risk under unusually fast moving technical and regulatory conditions. The objective is to compound value through durable cash flows and defensible capabilities, while avoiding narrative driven repricing that can occur when capital supply expands faster than real adoption and unit economics. This section translates the earlier risk indicators and technical reality checks into practical investment principles that can be applied across public equities, private venture, infrastructure, and credit.

### 9.1. Fundamentals-based investment criteria and long-term value indicators

Use fundamentals that connect AI capability to repeatable cash generation, and explicitly separate model performance from business performance.

Core criteria that signal long term value.
- Revenue quality and proof of willingness to pay, evidenced by multi year contracts, low churn, and expansion within existing accounts.
- Gross margin durability after inference costs, including sensitivity to model choice, token pricing, and hardware availability.
- Capital discipline and funding resilience, including runway under conservative growth assumptions and limited dependence on continuous mega rounds.
- Clear path to distribution, such as embedded channels, platform partnerships, or product led growth that does not rely on unsustainably high customer acquisition costs.
- Defensibility beyond access to frontier models, including proprietary data rights, workflow integration, switching costs, and compliance readiness.
- Operational readiness for regulated deployment, including auditability, security controls, and documented model risk management.
- Compute and energy realism, including credible procurement plans and exposure to power and data center constraints.

Long term value indicators by AI layer.
- Infrastructure and semiconductors.
  - Look for evidence of sustained demand tied to enterprise and hyperscaler capex, and for supply chain execution that converts backlog into revenue.
  - Prefer firms with pricing power supported by performance leadership and ecosystem lock in.
- Cloud and platforms.
  - Underwrite the ability to monetize AI through higher attach rates, consumption growth, and retention, rather than only through headline model launches.
- Applications.
  - Require measurable productivity or revenue uplift for customers, with payback periods that remain attractive even if model costs rise.

Title: Selected AI market and infrastructure indicators used in fundamentals screening

| Indicator | Value (units) | Period (end date) | Source |
|:---|---:|:---|:---|
| Global VC investment in generative AI | 56,000,000,000.00 USD | 2024-12-31 | [62](https://www.spglobal.com/market-intelligence/en/news-insights/articles/2025/1/genai-funding-hits-record-in-2024-boosted-by-infrastructure-interest-87132257) |
| Global VC investment in generative AI | 49,200,000,000.00 USD | 2025-06-30 | [61](https://www.ey.com/en_ie/newsroom/2025/06/generative-ai-vc-funding-49-2b-h1-2025-ey-report) |
| NVIDIA total revenue | 130,497,000,000.00 USD | 2025-01-26 | [63](https://nvidianews.nvidia.com/news/nvidia-announces-financial-results-for-fourth-quarter-and-fiscal-2025) |
| Global data centre electricity consumption projection | 945.00 TWh | 2030-12-31 | [28](https://www.iea.org/reports/energy-and-ai/energy-demand-from-ai) |

Source: Linked sources in the table.

Practical underwriting tests.
- Unit economics stress test.
  - Recalculate gross margin assuming higher inference costs, lower token pricing, and slower customer expansion.
- Concentration test.
  - Quantify dependence on a single model provider, cloud provider, or distribution partner, and require contractual protections where feasible.
- Adoption friction test.
  - Validate that deployment timelines account for security review, data integration, and regulatory approvals, not only model availability.
- Governance test.
  - Require board level oversight of AI risk, documented evaluation practices, and incident response plans for model failures and data leakage.

### 9.2. Differentiating speculative hype from durable AI value creation

Speculative phases often share a pattern of narrative dominance, rapid multiple expansion, and capital chasing scarce assets such as compute, talent, and perceived model advantage. Durable value creation is more consistently associated with measurable customer outcomes, repeatable distribution, and improving unit economics.

Signals of speculative hype.
- Valuations justified primarily by total addressable market narratives without evidence of conversion to paid usage.
- Growth driven by subsidies, free credits, or promotional pricing that masks true inference and support costs.
- Business models that assume continuous model performance leaps to unlock monetization, rather than monetization from current capabilities.
- Excessive dependence on a single hyperscaler or foundation model provider without credible multi vendor fallback.
- Aggressive leverage or debt funded expansion tied to uncertain AI revenue timelines.

Signals of durable AI value creation.
- Customers renew and expand at stable or improving gross margins after including model serving costs.
- The product becomes embedded in workflows, creating switching costs and measurable productivity gains.
- The firm can swap models or optimize inference without breaking the product, indicating architectural resilience.
- Compliance and security posture enables regulated customers to deploy at scale.

Due diligence strategies to separate hype from fundamentals.
- Demand evidence of realized value.
  - Require customer references that quantify time saved, error reduction, revenue lift, or risk reduction, and confirm that benefits persist after pilots.
- Verify AI claims and avoid AI washing risk.
  - Cross check marketing statements against technical documentation, model cards, and internal controls, noting that the SEC has already brought enforcement actions for misleading AI related claims by investment advisers[64](https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2024-186) .
- Underwrite the cost curve.
  - Model gross margin under multiple scenarios for compute pricing, model licensing, and utilization, and require a credible plan for optimization.
- Treat frontier model advantage as transient.
  - Prefer companies whose moat is data rights, distribution, and integration, because model capability gaps can compress quickly.

Decision rules that reduce bubble exposure.
- Do not pay for growth that is not financed by gross profit within a defined horizon.
- Require downside survivability, meaning the company can operate through a funding winter without forced asset sales.
- Prefer transparent reporting of AI specific metrics, such as inference cost per unit of output, latency, and reliability, because opacity is a common feature of hype cycles.

### 9.3. Portfolio diversification strategies within AI and adjacent sectors

Diversification in AI should be designed around distinct risk drivers, not only around company count. The key is to avoid a portfolio that is implicitly a single bet on one compute cycle, one model paradigm, or one regulatory regime.

Diversification dimensions that matter.
- Layer diversification.
  - Balance exposure across semiconductors and infrastructure, cloud platforms, and applications, because their cash flow timing and sensitivity to compute costs differ.
- Compute intensity diversification.
  - Combine compute heavy training and serving businesses with AI enabled software and services that monetize primarily through workflow integration.
- Geography and policy diversification.
  - Spread exposure across jurisdictions with different regulatory and industrial policy trajectories, while accounting for cross border restrictions on chips, data, and models.
- Customer sector diversification.
  - Avoid over concentration in a single end market such as advertising, consumer apps, or a single regulated vertical.

Practical portfolio construction approaches.
- Barbell approach.
  - Pair cash generative incumbents that monetize AI through existing distribution with smaller positions in higher volatility frontier bets.
- Infrastructure hedging.
  - Where AI application exposure is high, consider partial offsets through positions that benefit from rising AI utilization, such as power grid equipment, data centre cooling, and networking, while recognizing that these can also be cyclical.
- Liquidity tiering.
  - Match private AI venture exposure with sufficient liquid public holdings or cash equivalents to avoid forced selling during repricing events.
- Scenario based rebalancing.
  - Predefine triggers for trimming, such as when valuation expansion outpaces earnings revisions, or when capex plans accelerate faster than demonstrated AI revenue.

Risk controls specific to AI concentration.
