- Cap single name and single theme exposure, especially where returns are driven by the same hyperscaler capex cycle.
- Monitor systemic inputs, including the pace of AI infrastructure spending and the energy constraint trajectory, since data centre electricity demand is projected to rise rapidly through 2030[28](https://www.iea.org/reports/energy-and-ai/energy-demand-from-ai) .
- Maintain a contingency allocation for post correction opportunities, because bubble like phases can still produce durable winners at more sustainable entry prices.

## 10. Policy Recommendations for Market Stability

This section translates the earlier risk indicators and investor due diligence findings into concrete policy measures that reduce information asymmetry, limit procyclical leverage and capex cycles, and improve the ability of supervisors to detect and contain AI related market overheating before it becomes a broader financial stability event. The recommendations are designed to be globally interoperable, so that disclosures and stress tests can be compared across jurisdictions even when local rules differ.

### 10.1. Transparency and disclosure standards for AI investment and valuations

Policy objective.
Reduce mispricing risk by making AI specific value drivers auditable and comparable across issuers, funds, and infrastructure projects, with a focus on capex intensity, compute supply dependencies, and revenue quality.

Recommended disclosure baseline for public markets.
Require standardized AI exposure disclosures in annual and interim reporting for issuers that are materially dependent on AI revenue, AI capex, or AI supply chains.

Minimum disclosure elements.
Disclose AI related revenue composition by product category and customer concentration, including the share of revenue that is usage based versus fixed subscription.
Disclose AI related cost structure drivers, including compute costs, model training and inference costs, and material third party dependencies.
Disclose capex commitments and contractual obligations tied to data centers, accelerators, and long term cloud capacity reservations.
Disclose model and platform dependency risk, including reliance on a small number of foundation model providers or cloud providers.
Disclose governance and risk management alignment to recognized frameworks for AI risk, including the NIST AI Risk Management Framework and the NIST Generative AI Profile for organizations deploying generative AI at scale [56](https://www.nist.gov/publications/artificial-intelligence-risk-management-framework-ai-rmf-10).

Private market and fund level transparency.
Require large private funds and late stage private issuers marketing AI exposure to provide a standardized valuation memo that includes.
Comparable company set and rationale.
Unit economics assumptions and sensitivity ranges.
Compute and energy cost assumptions.
Customer retention and expansion assumptions.
Down round and liquidation preference waterfall analysis.

Valuation discipline guardrails.
Supervisors should encourage valuation committees to explicitly separate.
Narrative value, such as optionality and strategic positioning.
Cash flow value, such as contracted revenue and observable margins.
This reduces the risk that narrative dominance drives systematic overvaluation.

Disclosure interoperability and global alignment.
Use ISSB aligned sustainability disclosure as a cross border baseline for climate and energy related inputs that materially affect AI infrastructure economics, especially where data center power sourcing and emissions are financially material [65](https://www.spglobal.com/sustainable1/en/insights/march-2025-where-does-the-world-stand-on-issb-adoption).
In the EU, align AI infrastructure disclosures with the evolving CSRD and ESRS reporting perimeter and timelines, noting that EU institutions have adopted a stop the clock delay and are pursuing scope simplification that changes which firms must report and when [66](https://www.consilium.europa.eu/en/press/press-releases/2025/06/23/simplification-council-agrees-position-on-sustainability-reporting-and-due-diligence-requirements-to-boost-eu-competitiveness/).
In the United States, avoid assuming SEC climate disclosure rules will supply comparable energy and emissions data, because the SEC voted in March 2025 to end its defense of the 2024 climate disclosure rules and the rules had previously been stayed pending litigation [67](https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2025-58).

Implementation mechanisms.
Adopt a comply or explain approach for AI specific disclosures in the near term, then migrate to mandatory line item disclosures once definitions stabilize.
Use digital tagging for AI specific disclosures to enable automated supervisory monitoring and market analytics.

Title: Selected disclosure regimes affecting AI infrastructure transparency as of 2025.12.31

| Regime or standard | Status date | Coverage focus | Reporting start or effective date |
|:---|---:|:---|---:|
| SEC climate disclosure rules | 2025.03.27 | Climate risk and emissions disclosures for public companies | Stayed and SEC ended defense in litigation |
| EU CSRD | 2025.06.23 | Corporate sustainability reporting using ESRS | Delayed for some entities by 2.00 years under stop the clock |
| ISSB IFRS S1 and IFRS S2 | 2025.03.31 | Global baseline sustainability disclosures | 15.00 jurisdictions reporting from 2024.01.01 or 2025.01.01 |

Source: SEC press release 2025 58 [67](https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2025-58). EU Council press release on Omnibus and stop the clock [66](https://www.consilium.europa.eu/en/press/press-releases/2025/06/23/simplification-council-agrees-position-on-sustainability-reporting-and-due-diligence-requirements-to-boost-eu-competitiveness/). S and P Global summary of ISSB adoption status as of 2025.03.31 [65](https://www.spglobal.com/sustainable1/en/insights/march-2025-where-does-the-world-stand-on-issb-adoption).

### 10.2. Stress-testing frameworks tailored to AI infrastructure and business models

Policy objective.
Develop repeatable, comparable stress tests that capture AI specific fragilities that are weakly represented in traditional macro stress testing. Prioritise compute supply shocks, power and grid constraints, and rapid margin compression driven by model commoditisation.

Scope.
Apply to.
- Systemically relevant AI infrastructure operators, including large data centre developers and operators with concentrated AI tenant exposure.
- Large cloud and platform firms where AI capital expenditure and AI revenue are material.
- Banks and non bank lenders with concentrated exposure to AI infrastructure finance.
- Large venture and growth funds with high AI concentration.

Core AI stress scenarios.
1. Compute price and availability shock.
   - Assume a rapid increase in accelerator prices and or reduced availability due to supply chain disruption.
   - Transmission channels include capital expenditure inflation, delayed capacity delivery, and covenant pressure.
2. Power and grid constraint shock.
   - Assume delayed interconnection, higher power prices, and or curtailment that reduces utilisation of built capacity.
   - Transmission channels include lower revenue per deployed megawatt, stranded fit out, and higher operating costs.
3. Demand normalisation shock.
   - Assume slower enterprise adoption and lower inference utilisation than the base case.
   - Transmission channels include revenue shortfall, inventory of unused capacity, and valuation resets.
4. Model commoditisation shock.
   - Assume rapid erosion of pricing power for model access and AI features.
   - Transmission channels include margin compression, customer churn, and repricing of long term contracts.
5. Regulatory and compliance cost shock.
   - Assume higher compliance costs and slower deployment cycles in regulated sectors.
   - Transmission channels include delayed revenue recognition, higher cost to serve, and reduced addressable demand.
6. Cyber and operational disruption shock.
   - Assume a material outage at a concentrated third party provider.
   - Transmission channels include correlated downtime losses, contractual penalties, and liquidity drawdowns.

Metrics and outputs.
Require standardised outputs to enable cross firm comparison and supervisory aggregation.
- Liquidity runway under stressed capital expenditure and working capital.
- Debt service coverage ratio under utilisation and pricing shocks.
- Capital expenditure deferral capacity, including break clauses and termination rights in long term commitments.
- Customer concentration sensitivity, including top customer revenue share and contract repricing exposure.
- Third party concentration sensitivity, including dependency mapping to critical providers and substitutability.

Supervisory integration.
- Embed AI stress tests into operational resilience and third party risk supervision, leveraging the Basel Committee principles based approach to operational resilience, including governance, tolerance for disruption, and third party dependency management expectations[37](https://www.bis.org/fsi/fsisummaries/op_resilience.htm) .
- Use the Financial Stability Board framing of AI related vulnerabilities as a common taxonomy for supervisory data collection and scenario design, including third party dependencies and service provider concentration, market correlations, cyber risk, and model risk[34](https://www.fsb.org/2024/11/fsb-assesses-the-financial-stability-implications-of-artificial-intelligence/) .

System wide exercises.
Encourage central banks and financial authorities to run periodic system wide exploratory scenarios that include market based finance channels and concentrated technology exposures, building on the Bank of England system wide exploratory scenario exercise approach focused on system wide dynamics and amplification effects rather than firm pass fail outcomes[38](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/financial-stability/boe-system-wide-exploratory-scenario-exercise/boe-swes) .

Banking sector linkage.
Where banks have material exposure to AI infrastructure lending, incorporate AI specific overlays into existing adverse macro scenarios used in bank stress tests.
- For example, the European Banking Authority 2025 EU wide stress test uses a baseline and adverse scenario over a 3 year horizon from 2025 to 2027, and the adverse scenario includes a cumulative GDP decline of 6.3% across the horizon. This structure can be extended with sector overlays for AI infrastructure and technology capital expenditure supply chains[39](https://www.eba.europa.eu/publications-and-media/press-releases/eba-launches-its-2025-eu-wide-stress-test) .

Title: Example AI stress overlays mapped to supervisory toolkits

| Stress overlay | Primary transmission channel | Example metric |
|:---|:---|---:|
| Compute supply shock | Capital expenditure inflation and delayed capacity | Debt service coverage ratio under capital expenditure increase ratio |
| Power constraint shock | Lower utilisation and stranded assets | Utilisation rate under curtailment % |
| Third party outage shock | Operational disruption and correlated losses | Revenue at risk from top 3 providers % |
| Demand normalisation shock | Revenue shortfall and valuation reset | Liquidity runway months |


![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Source: Basel Committee Principles for operational resilience executive summary[37](https://www.bis.org/fsi/fsisummaries/op_resilience.htm) . Financial Stability Board The Financial Stability Implications of Artificial Intelligence[13](https://www.fsb.org/2024/11/the-financial-stability-implications-of-artificial-intelligence/) . Bank of England system wide exploratory scenario exercise description[38](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/financial-stability/boe-system-wide-exploratory-scenario-exercise/boe-swes) . European Banking Authority 2025 EU wide stress test press release[39](https://www.eba.europa.eu/publications-and-media/press-releases/eba-launches-its-2025-eu-wide-stress-test) .

### 10.3. Phased regulatory implementation and adaptive oversight approaches

Policy objective.
Reduce bubble amplification from abrupt rule changes while still closing key supervisory blind spots, using phased implementation, proportionality, and rapid feedback loops.

Phased implementation model.
Phase 1 baseline visibility.
Introduce a minimum AI exposure disclosure package and supervisory data collection for large issuers, large funds, and critical infrastructure operators.
Use comply or explain to accelerate adoption while definitions mature.
Phase 2 targeted prudential overlays.
Apply concentration and liquidity risk overlays where exposures are demonstrably system relevant, such as high leverage data center financing or concentrated cloud dependency.
Phase 3 harmonization and enforcement.
Move from guidance to enforceable standards once metrics and audit practices stabilize.

Adaptive oversight mechanisms.
Regulatory sandboxes for disclosure and assurance.
Pilot AI specific disclosure templates and assurance procedures with a limited set of firms, then scale.
Dynamic materiality thresholds.
Update thresholds for what constitutes material AI exposure based on market concentration and systemic linkages.
Supervisory technology.
Use machine readable filings and anomaly detection to flag rapid changes in capex commitments, customer concentration, or valuation assumptions.

Cross border coordination.
Promote interoperability rather than identical rules.
Common definitions for AI capex, AI revenue, and compute commitments.
Common taxonomy for third party concentration and operational resilience.
Mutual recognition pathways for assurance standards.

Policy sequencing with sustainability reporting regimes.
In the EU, coordinate AI infrastructure transparency requirements with the evolving CSRD perimeter and timing, recognizing that EU institutions have pursued simplification and delayed certain reporting start dates, which can otherwise create data gaps during periods of rapid AI capex expansion [66](https://www.consilium.europa.eu/en/press/press-releases/2025/06/23/simplification-council-agrees-position-on-sustainability-reporting-and-due-diligence-requirements-to-boost-eu-competitiveness/).
In the United States, plan for a fragmented disclosure environment on energy and emissions inputs relevant to AI infrastructure economics, given the SEC decision in March 2025 to end its defense of the 2024 climate disclosure rules [67](https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2025-58).

Contingency triggers for tightening or loosening.
Define ex ante triggers that adjust supervisory intensity.
Rapid growth in AI infrastructure leverage.
Sharp increases in long term take or pay compute commitments.
Rising concentration in a small number of compute or model providers.
Evidence of widespread valuation practices that rely on untested adoption assumptions.

Institutional capacity building.
Create dedicated AI market stability units inside financial authorities that combine.
Capital markets expertise.
Technology and operational resilience expertise.
Data science for supervisory analytics.
This aligns with the Financial Stability Board recommendation to enhance monitoring and supervisory capabilities as AI adoption accelerates [69](https://www.fsb.org/2024/11/fsb-assesses-the-financial-stability-implications-of-artificial-intelligence/).

## 11. Central Bank and Financial Authority Considerations

An AI bubble, if it forms, is most likely to transmit to the real economy through financial conditions rather than through immediate destruction of productive capacity. For central banks and financial authorities, the key challenge is to distinguish productivity driven repricing from narrative driven duration risk, and to contain amplification channels such as leverage, liquidity mismatch, and concentrated exposures in both banks and nonbanks. Recent official sector assessments emphasize that stretched valuations and market concentration can raise the probability of sharp corrections, while the growing role of nonbank financial intermediation can accelerate stress transmission across markets and into the banking system [72](https://www.ecb.europa.eu/press/financial-stability-publications/fsr/html/ecb.fsr202511~263b5810d4.en.html).

### 11.1. Monetary policy implications of AI-driven asset inflation

AI driven asset inflation can complicate monetary policy by loosening financial conditions even when inflation is not yet durably at target. The primary transmission mechanisms are the wealth effect and the cost of capital channel. When equity valuations rise in AI exposed firms, household net worth increases and firms can finance investment more cheaply through higher equity prices and tighter credit spreads. This can support consumption and investment, increase risk taking, and slow the pace of disinflation, thereby reducing the scope for policy easing.

The IMF has explicitly warned that asset prices can become stretched and therefore vulnerable to valuation readjustment, raising the risk of sharp corrections that tighten financial conditions abruptly[37](https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/GFSR/Issues/2025/10/14/global-financial-stability-report-october-2025) .

Key monetary policy considerations in an AI bubble risk environment include.

- Financial conditions can ease endogenously through equity repricing and easier credit availability even if the policy rate is unchanged, requiring greater weight on broad financial conditions measures and risk premia rather than the policy rate alone.
- Equity market concentration can increase the macro sensitivity of household wealth and corporate financing to a small set of firms, increasing tail risk if AI expectations reset and valuations reprice quickly.
- AI infrastructure investment cycles can create procyclical demand impulses through capex and related build out, which can interact with inflation dynamics and complicate output gap assessment.
- Central banks should avoid using the policy rate to target specific asset prices, but should incorporate bubble risk into a risk management approach to policy, including scenario analysis for abrupt repricing and its impact on credit supply, investment, and employment.

Title: Selected indicators for AI driven asset inflation monitoring

| Indicator | Value (units) | Date | Source |
|:---|---:|:---|:---|
| Global VC investment in generative AI | 49,200,000,000.00 USD | 2025-06-30 | EY[42](https://www.ey.com/en_ie/newsroom/2025/06/generative-ai-vc-funding-49-2b-h1-2025-ey-report) . |
| US share of global generative AI deal value | 97.000% | 2025-06-30 | EY[42](https://www.ey.com/en_ie/newsroom/2025/06/generative-ai-vc-funding-49-2b-h1-2025-ey-report) . |
| Magnificent 7 share of S and P 500 market cap | 34.400% | 2026-01-02 | The Motley Fool[40](https://www.fool.com/research/magnificent-seven-sp-500/) . |
| Magnificent 7 price increase since 2025-04-08 | 58.000% | 2025-11-01 | ECB Financial Stability Review November 2025[41](https://www.ecb.europa.eu/press/financial-stability-publications/fsr/html/ecb.fsr202511~263b5810d4.cs.html) . |


![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Source: EY Generative AI Key Deals and Market Insights, IMF Global Financial Stability Report October 2025, ECB Financial Stability Review November 2025, and The Motley Fool market cap compilation[42](https://www.ey.com/en_ie/newsroom/2025/06/generative-ai-vc-funding-49-2b-h1-2025-ey-report) .

Operational guidance for central banks.

- Strengthen integration of equity concentration and duration risk into macro financial forecasting, including stress paths where a small set of AI exposed firms drives a large share of the equity drawdown.
- Communicate clearly that monetary policy is set for inflation and employment objectives, while financial stability tools address leverage and liquidity risks, to reduce moral hazard and expectations of a central bank put.
- Coordinate with prudential authorities to reduce the risk that an AI repricing triggers disorderly deleveraging through margin spirals, liquidity mismatches, or procyclical risk limits in banks and nonbanks.

### 11.2. Systemic risk monitoring and containment strategies for AI markets

Systemic risk from AI markets is less about the technology itself and more about correlated positioning, leverage, and interconnected balance sheets across banks, broker dealers, funds, and private credit vehicles financing AI related capex and growth. Official sector monitoring has increasingly emphasized vulnerabilities from stretched valuations and the growing role of nonbank financial intermediaries, including the potential for stress to transmit quickly into core banking through counterparty and market channels [75](https://www.fsb.org/2025/12/fsb-reports-continued-growth-in-nonbank-financial-intermediation-in-2024-to-258-trillion/).

Priority monitoring domains for supervisors and systemic risk councils.
- Concentration and correlation mapping.
  - Track index concentration, passive flows, and factor exposures to AI linked equities and semiconductors.
  - Monitor cross asset correlation shifts during stress, especially equity volatility spilling into credit spreads and funding markets.
- Leverage and liquidity mismatch in nonbanks.
  - Expand data collection on hedge fund leverage, total return swaps, and prime brokerage exposures linked to AI equity baskets.
  - Monitor open ended fund liquidity transformation and margin preparedness, consistent with the FSB focus on NBFI vulnerabilities and leverage related recommendations [76](https://www.fsb.org/2025/07/fsb-publishes-recommendations-to-address-financial-stability-risks-created-by-leverage-in-nonbank-financial-intermediation/).
- Bank exposures to AI capex and infrastructure.
  - Identify direct lending and underwriting exposures to data center developers, power and grid upgrades, and semiconductor supply chains.
  - Stress test covenant quality and refinancing risk under scenarios of demand normalization and higher funding costs.
- Operational and model risk in financial institutions using AI.
  - Supervisors should treat widespread AI adoption in credit, trading, and claims processing as a potential amplifier of herding and procyclicality, including synchronized model responses during shocks.
  - Recent UK parliamentary scrutiny has highlighted concerns that AI use across financial firms could create systemic vulnerabilities if not stress tested and governed appropriately [77](https://www.theguardian.com/business/2026/jan/20/uk-ai-risks-mps-government-bank-of-england-fca).

Containment and resilience measures that do not require targeting AI valuations directly.
- Macroprudential buffers and targeted capital tools.
  - Use countercyclical capital buffers and sectoral capital requirements where exposures to AI linked credit and infrastructure become systemically material.
- Margin and collateral resilience.
  - Require robust margining and liquidity preparedness for nonbanks and their leverage providers, aligned with the FSB emphasis on containing leverage driven amplification in core markets [76](https://www.fsb.org/2025/07/fsb-publishes-recommendations-to-address-financial-stability-risks-created-by-leverage-in-nonbank-financial-intermediation/).
- Supervisory stress testing tailored to AI bubble transmission.
  - Equity shock scenarios concentrated in a small set of mega cap firms.
  - Credit spread widening for AI infrastructure borrowers and private credit funds.
  - Funding stress in repo and prime brokerage channels.
  - Cyber and operational disruption scenarios affecting cloud and AI service providers that are critical third parties.
- Market wide circuit breakers and liquidity backstops planning.
  - Pre plan operational playbooks for disorderly market functioning episodes, while maintaining clear eligibility and pricing to limit moral hazard.

Title: Nonbank financial intermediation scale relevant to AI bubble transmission channels.

| Metric | Value | Date | Source |
|:---|---:|:---|:---|
| NBFI share of total global financial assets units percent | 51.000% | 2024-12-31 | [75](https://www.fsb.org/2025/12/fsb-reports-continued-growth-in-nonbank-financial-intermediation-in-2024-to-258-trillion/) |
| NBFI total financial assets units USD | 256,800,000,000,000.00 USD | 2024-12-31 | [75](https://www.fsb.org/2025/12/fsb-reports-continued-growth-in-nonbank-financial-intermediation-in-2024-to-258-trillion/) |
| NBFI growth rate units percent | 9.400% | 2024-12-31 | [75](https://www.fsb.org/2025/12/fsb-reports-continued-growth-in-nonbank-financial-intermediation-in-2024-to-258-trillion/) |
| Banking sector growth rate units percent | 4.700% | 2024-12-31 | [75](https://www.fsb.org/2025/12/fsb-reports-continued-growth-in-nonbank-financial-intermediation-in-2024-to-258-trillion/) |

Source: Financial Stability Board Global Monitoring Report on Nonbank Financial Intermediation covering 2024 [75](https://www.fsb.org/2025/12/fsb-reports-continued-growth-in-nonbank-financial-intermediation-in-2024-to-258-trillion/).

### 11.3. Cross-border coordination on AI investment and financial stability

AI investment and valuation dynamics are inherently cross border because.
- Equity repricing in a small set of globally held firms transmits through passive funds and benchmarked mandates worldwide.
- AI supply chains span semiconductors, cloud infrastructure, energy equipment, and data center construction across multiple jurisdictions.
- Nonbank intermediation and private markets often operate through global fund structures, creating data gaps and regulatory perimeter challenges.

International coordination priorities.
- Harmonize AI exposure and concentration reporting for systemic monitoring.
  - Establish a common minimum dataset for AI related exposures across banks, insurers, funds, and major corporates, including derivatives and prime brokerage exposures.
  - Use interoperable identifiers for AI infrastructure projects and counterparties to support cross jurisdiction aggregation.
- Coordinate oversight of nonbank leverage and liquidity risks.
  - The FSB has emphasized both the scale of NBFI and the need to address leverage related vulnerabilities, and it has also highlighted data limitations for private credit, which is directly relevant to AI infrastructure financing [76](https://www.fsb.org/2025/07/fsb-publishes-recommendations-to-address-financial-stability-risks-created-by-leverage-in-nonbank-financial-intermediation/).
- Align stress testing scenarios and disclosure templates.
  - Agree on a shared set of AI bubble stress scenarios for supervisory exercises, including concentrated equity drawdowns, funding stress, and AI infrastructure demand normalization.
  - Promote mutual recognition of assurance approaches for AI related financial disclosures to reduce regulatory arbitrage.
- Coordinate on critical third party and cloud concentration risk.
  - Develop joint supervisory protocols for outages, cyber incidents, and operational disruptions at globally systemic cloud and AI service providers.
  - Ensure that cross border resolution and continuity planning includes technology service dependencies.
- Establish crisis communication and market functioning playbooks.
  - Pre agree communication principles among central banks and market regulators to reduce uncertainty during a rapid repricing episode.
  - Coordinate temporary measures for market functioning only when necessary, with consistent messaging to avoid cross border spillovers.

Global perspective on why coordination is urgent.
- The ECB has documented that the US equity rebound in 2025 was driven primarily by technology and AI related firms, increasing concentration and reviving early 2000s parallels, which implies that a US centered repricing can quickly become a global financial conditions shock [72](https://www.ecb.europa.eu/press/financial-stability-publications/fsr/html/ecb.fsr202511~263b5810d4.en.html).
- The IMF has warned that stretched valuations and the growing role of nonbanks can leave markets vulnerable to sharp corrections and rapid transmission into core institutions, reinforcing the need for coordinated surveillance and policy responses [73](https://www.imf.org/en/publications/gfsr/issues/2025/10/14/global-financial-stability-report-october-2025).

Recommended institutional mechanisms.
- Use the Financial Stability Board as the primary venue for coordinating NBFI leverage and data initiatives, with implementation tracked through peer reviews and supervisory dialogues [76](https://www.fsb.org/2025/07/fsb-publishes-recommendations-to-address-financial-stability-risks-created-by-leverage-in-nonbank-financial-intermediation/).
- Use BIS hosted central bank forums to align macro financial scenario design and cross border liquidity stress playbooks, especially where AI related equity concentration and funding markets interact [78](https://www.bis.org/about/areport/areport2025.htm).
- Integrate AI bubble transmission scenarios into IMF surveillance and FSAP workstreams to support consistent cross country diagnostics and policy advice [73](https://www.imf.org/en/publications/gfsr/issues/2025/10/14/global-financial-stability-report-october-2025).

## 12. Investor Protection Frameworks

Investor protection in AI linked markets should be treated as a market integrity and financial stability objective, not only a consumer education issue. Because AI narratives can amplify valuation dispersion and because AI business models often rely on opaque technical claims, third party dependencies, and rapidly shifting unit economics, investor protection frameworks should prioritize verifiable disclosures, suitability aligned distribution, and enforcement that deters AI washing and AI enabled fraud. In practice, this requires coordinated action across securities regulators, self regulatory organizations, auditors, and market infrastructure providers to reduce information asymmetry and to make AI related risks legible to both retail and institutional investors.

### 12.1. Safeguards for retail and institutional investors in AI markets

Retail investor safeguards should focus on preventing mis selling, limiting exposure to products whose risks cannot be reasonably explained, and ensuring that AI related claims are substantiated and balanced.

Retail focused safeguards.
- Require that any AI themed product marketing be clearly identifiable as marketing and present risks and rewards with equal prominence, consistent with EU expectations for fund marketing communications and the clear fair not misleading standard. This is particularly important for AI thematic ETFs, AI tokenized products, and AI linked structured notes marketed through social media channels. Citation[79](https://www.esma.europa.eu/press-news/esma-news/esma-publishes-guidance-funds%E2%80%99-marketing-communications) .
- Apply technology neutral supervision to AI enabled brokerage and advisory workflows, including supervision of generative AI used in customer communications, suitability processes, and surveillance. FINRA has explicitly reminded member firms that existing rules continue to apply when firms use generative AI and large language models, including supervision and governance expectations. Citation[80](https://www.finra.org/rules-guidance/notices/24-09) .
- Strengthen privacy and incident response protections for retail clients whose data may be used to train or operate AI systems in advisory and brokerage contexts. In the United States, the SEC Regulation S P amendments require written incident response programs and customer notification within 30 days after becoming aware of certain unauthorized access or use of sensitive customer information. Citation[81](https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2024-58) .
- Require explicit disclosure when retail recommendations are materially influenced by automated decision systems, including the role of third party models, the key input features, and the limits of explainability. ESMA has issued guidance for firms using AI in investment services to retail clients, emphasizing MiFID II obligations to act in the best interest of the client and highlighting risks such as bias, opacity, overreliance, and privacy and security concerns. Citation[82](https://www.esma.europa.eu/press-news/esma-news/esma-provides-guidance-firms-using-artificial-intelligence-investment-services) .

Institutional investor safeguards should focus on governance, model risk management, concentration and counterparty risk, and enforceable information rights.
- Mandate AI specific investment governance for fiduciaries, including documented model risk management for any AI used in research, portfolio construction, or trading, and documented oversight of third party AI vendors. This aligns with FINRA guidance that firms should address technology governance, data privacy and integrity, and reliability and accuracy when using generative AI tools. Citation[80](https://www.finra.org/rules-guidance/notices/24-09) .
- Require enhanced due diligence and ongoing monitoring for AI infrastructure exposures, including cloud concentration, data center capex commitments, and energy procurement risks, with board level reporting for material exposures.
- For private markets, standardize investor protection terms that reduce information asymmetry.
  - Minimum information rights for limited partners, including quarterly reporting of AI revenue attribution, compute and cloud spend, and model dependency risks.
  - Side letter parity or most favored nation clauses for material risk disclosures.
  - Independent valuation policies for AI heavy portfolios, including documented treatment of compute credits, revenue sharing, and related party transactions.

Operational safeguards that apply to both retail and institutional channels.
- Cyber and data governance as investor protection. The SEC Regulation S P amendments create a baseline expectation for incident response and notification that should be treated as a minimum standard for AI enabled financial firms, given the sensitivity of customer data and the potential for AI enabled identity fraud. Citation[81](https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2024-58) .
- Examination and supervision focus on AI claims and controls. The SEC Division of Examinations has identified artificial intelligence as an examination priority area in its 2025 priorities and continues to focus on emerging financial technology in its 2026 priorities, supporting a supervisory posture that tests whether representations are accurate and whether controls match disclosures. Citations[83](https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2024-172) . [84](https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2025-132-sec-division-examinations-announces-2026-priorities).

Title: SEC Regulation S P compliance dates and notification requirement.

| Requirement or milestone | Value | Unit | Source |
|:---|---:|:---:|:---|
| Customer notification deadline after awareness of certain incidents | 30 | Days | [81](https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2024-58) |
| Compliance date for larger entities | 12/03/2025 | Date | [85](https://www.finra.org/rules-guidance/guidance/sec-regulation-s-p-compliance-date-reminder-20251114) |
| Compliance date for smaller entities | 06/03/2026 | Date | [85](https://www.finra.org/rules-guidance/guidance/sec-regulation-s-p-compliance-date-reminder-20251114) |

Source: SEC Regulation S P amendments press release and FINRA compliance date reminder[81](https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2024-58) . [85](https://www.finra.org/rules-guidance/guidance/sec-regulation-s-p-compliance-date-reminder-20251114).

### 12.2. Reducing information asymmetry and enhancing disclosure clarity

AI markets are unusually prone to information asymmetry because value drivers are technical, fast moving, and often embedded in third party dependencies. Investor protection frameworks should therefore require disclosures that are decision useful, comparable, and auditable, and should penalize ambiguous AI narratives that cannot be substantiated.

Disclosure clarity principles for AI related offerings.
- Substantiation by design. Any claim about AI capabilities, AI driven performance, or AI enabled risk reduction should be supported by documentation that can be produced on demand to regulators and investors. The SEC Investment Adviser Marketing Rule prohibits untrue statements and requires a reasonable basis for substantiation of material statements of fact in advertisements. Citation[86](https://www.sec.gov/investment/investment-adviser-marketing) .
- Balanced presentation. Marketing and investor communications should present benefits and material risks with equal prominence, consistent with ESMA guidance for fund marketing communications. Citation[79](https://www.esma.europa.eu/press-news/esma-news/esma-publishes-guidance-funds%E2%80%99-marketing-communications) .
- Plain language AI exposure mapping. Require a standardized AI exposure note that separates.
  - AI revenue that is directly attributable to AI products from revenue that is only AI adjacent.
  - AI costs that are variable with usage, such as inference and cloud spend, from fixed commitments such as long term data center leases and power purchase agreements.
  - Dependencies on specific model providers, cloud providers, and data licensors.

Minimum AI specific disclosure elements that reduce asymmetry.
- Model and data dependency disclosure.
  - Material third party model dependencies and switching costs.
  - Data provenance and licensing constraints that could impair scaling.
- Compute and infrastructure economics disclosure.
  - Current and contracted compute capacity, major counterparties, and termination clauses.
  - Sensitivity of gross margin to compute price changes and to model efficiency improvements.
- Governance and controls disclosure.
  - Whether AI is used in customer facing recommendations, and how human oversight is implemented.
  - Testing for bias, drift, and hallucination where relevant to investment decisions.
  - Cyber and privacy controls, including incident response readiness.

Machine readable and assurance oriented reporting.
- Encourage machine readable filings for AI exposure metrics to enable cross issuer comparability and automated surveillance.
- Require limited assurance for selected AI metrics where feasible, similar to how assurance is used for other non financial disclosures, to reduce narrative driven reporting.

Investor education that is tied to disclosure.
- Regulators and exchanges should publish standardized investor education materials that map common AI claims to the specific disclosures investors should demand, such as what constitutes evidence of AI use versus marketing language.
- Investor alerts should be integrated into onboarding flows for AI themed products and AI trading platforms. The SEC, NASAA, and FINRA have issued an investor alert warning about AI related investment fraud and unrealistic claims such as guaranteed returns. Citation[87](https://www.investor.gov/introduction-investing/general-resources/news-alerts/alerts-bulletins/investor-alerts/artificial-intelligence-fraud) .

Supervisory testing to reduce asymmetry.
- Examinations should test consistency between AI claims and operational reality, including whether firms actually use the AI described and whether controls match disclosures. The SEC Division of Examinations has explicitly highlighted AI and emerging financial technologies as examination focus areas in recent priorities. Citations[83](https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2024-172) . [84](https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2025-132-sec-division-examinations-announces-2026-priorities).

### 12.3. Fraud prevention and enforcement in AI-related investment offerings

Fraud risk in AI markets is amplified by complexity, by the ease of generating persuasive synthetic content, and by the speed at which narratives propagate through social media. Investor protection frameworks should combine preventive controls, rapid detection, and credible enforcement, with special attention to AI washing and AI enabled impersonation.

Preventive mechanisms.
- Registration and licensing verification by default.
  - Require platforms and intermediaries to present prominent registration status and regulator identifiers, and to provide direct links to official registries.
  - Encourage investors to verify registration before investing, consistent with the SEC, NASAA, and FINRA investor alert on AI related fraud. Citation[87](https://www.investor.gov/introduction-investing/general-resources/news-alerts/alerts-bulletins/investor-alerts/artificial-intelligence-fraud) .
- Marketing controls for AI claims.
  - Treat AI capability claims as material statements requiring substantiation under the SEC Marketing Rule framework for investment advisers. Citation[86](https://www.sec.gov/investment/investment-adviser-marketing) .
  - Require pre approval and compliance sign off for AI related marketing language, including social media posts and influencer content.
- Platform level scam friction.
  - Implement cooling off periods and risk acknowledgements for high volatility AI themed products.
  - Require step up verification for withdrawals and account changes to mitigate AI enabled social engineering.

Detection and monitoring.
- Supervisory analytics for AI enabled fraud patterns.
  - Monitor for unrealistic performance claims, guaranteed returns, and rapid escalation of deposits.
  - Monitor for impersonation patterns and synthetic identity signals.
- Cross market intelligence sharing.
  - Expand regulator and self regulatory organization information sharing on AI scams and impersonation, including shared blocklists of domains and wallet addresses where relevant.
  - Use public alert lists to warn investors about unregistered soliciting entities and impersonators. The SEC maintains and updates its PAUSE list of unregistered soliciting entities and impersonators. Citation[88](https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2024-76) .

Enforcement priorities and deterrence.
- Target AI washing as a core market integrity issue.
  - The SEC has brought enforcement actions against investment advisers for false and misleading statements about their use of AI, signaling that exaggerated AI claims can violate anti fraud and marketing rules. Citations[64](https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2024-186) . [89](https://corpgov.law.harvard.edu/2024/04/18/decoding-the-secs-first-ai-washing-enforcement-actions/).
- Align examinations with enforcement.
  - The SEC Division of Examinations has highlighted AI and emerging financial technologies as examination focus areas, supporting a pipeline from examination findings to enforcement where misrepresentations or control failures are identified. Citations[83](https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2024-172) . [84](https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2025-132-sec-division-examinations-announces-2026-priorities).
- Increase consequences for AI enabled impersonation and solicitation fraud.
  - Prioritize cases involving deepfake impersonation of regulators, executives, or licensed professionals, and require restitution focused remedies where possible.

Title: Quantified enforcement and fraud indicators relevant to AI related investor protection.

| Indicator | Value | Unit | Source |
|:---|---:|:---:|:---|
| SEC penalties in 03/18/2024 AI washing settlements for 2 investment advisers | 400,000.00 USD | Currency | [89](https://corpgov.law.harvard.edu/2024/04/18/decoding-the-secs-first-ai-washing-enforcement-actions/) |
| FBI reported losses in 2024 referenced by FINRA | 16,600,000,000.00 USD | Currency | [90](https://www.finra.org/rules-guidance/notices/26-02) |
| Increase in FBI reported losses in 2024 versus 2023 referenced by FINRA | 33.0% | Percent | [90](https://www.finra.org/rules-guidance/notices/26-02) |

Source: SEC related analysis of AI washing enforcement and FINRA notice citing FBI reported losses[89](https://corpgov.law.harvard.edu/2024/04/18/decoding-the-secs-first-ai-washing-enforcement-actions/) . [90](https://www.finra.org/rules-guidance/notices/26-02).

## 13. Case Studies of Effective Bubble Mitigation

This section translates historical bubble mitigation experience into practical lessons for the AI sector, focusing on interventions that reduced leverage, improved transparency, and slowed reflexive capital cycles without halting innovation. The case studies emphasize three recurring success factors.
- Early action that targets amplification mechanisms such as leverage, maturity mismatch, and opaque exposures.
- Rules that improve comparability of risk and reduce information asymmetry, especially where narratives can dominate fundamentals.
- Adaptive implementation that tightens during booms and relaxes during stress, reducing procyclicality.

For AI, the closest analogues are not only equity valuation episodes, but also infrastructure and credit cycles where capital intensity and long duration expectations can create overshoot. The most transferable lessons are to constrain leverage and maturity mismatch around AI linked assets, standardize disclosures for AI revenue and capex commitments, and use countercyclical tools that can be tightened or released as conditions change.

### 13.1. Historical examples of successful bubble avoidance or mitigation

Hong Kong property market macroprudential tightening as a leverage brake.
- Context and risk channel.
  - Hong Kong faced repeated property upcycles with high household leverage risk and bank exposure.
  - The policy objective was not to set prices, but to reduce systemic vulnerability by limiting borrower leverage and improving underwriting resilience.
- Measures used.
  - Loan to value caps and debt servicing ratio limits were tightened in multiple rounds starting in 2009, with additional supervisory measures such as positive mortgage data sharing to reduce hidden leverage and multiple borrowing across banks.
- Evidence of mitigation effects.
  - The Hong Kong Monetary Authority reported that average loan to value on new residential mortgages fell to around 55% from 64% in September 2009, and debt servicing ratios fell to 35% from 41% after tightening in 2010.
  - Growth in outstanding residential mortgage loans slowed to 4.2% annualized since the second quarter of 2013, compared with 8.7% since 2010.
  - These outcomes were presented by the HKMA as evidence that credit growth and leverage were brought under better control, improving resilience to a potential price correction.
  - Source for the quantitative outcomes and policy rationale is the HKMA financial stability discussion of its countercyclical measures.

Title: Hong Kong mortgage risk metrics reported after macroprudential tightening

| Metric | Before tightening value | After tightening value | Reference period | 
|:---|---:|---:|:---|
| Average LTV ratio on new residential mortgages percent | 64.0% | 55.0% | September 2009 vs recent months in 2014. |
| Average DSR on new residential mortgages percent | 41.0% | 35.0% | August 2010 vs recent months in 2014. |
| Annualized growth in outstanding residential mortgage loans percent | 8.7% | 4.2% | Since 2010 vs since Q2 2013. |


![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Source: [91](https://www.hkma.gov.hk/eng/news-and-media/insight/2014/07/20140714/).

Transferable lessons for AI bubble mitigation.
- Target leverage and underwriting standards around AI linked exposures, not just valuations.
  - For AI infrastructure, analogues include project finance leverage, vendor financing, and long term take or pay contracts that can embed hidden leverage.
- Use countercyclical design.
  - Tighten during rapid expansion and relax when conditions normalize, to avoid forcing fire sales or abrupt investment stops.
- Close information loopholes.
  - The positive mortgage data sharing lesson maps to AI as standardized disclosure of compute commitments, cloud concentration, and customer contract durability to prevent hidden correlated exposures.

Basel III countercyclical capital buffer as a system wide shock absorber.
- Context and risk channel.
  - The global financial crisis highlighted that bank capital was too procyclical, amplifying booms and busts.
- Measure used.
  - Basel III introduced a countercyclical capital buffer that can vary between 0% and 2.5% of risk weighted assets, intended to be built up in periods of excessive credit growth and released in stress.
- Why it matters for AI.
  - Even if AI risk is concentrated in nonbank channels, banks still provide credit to data center developers, equipment supply chains, and corporate borrowers whose cash flows depend on AI demand.
  - A countercyclical buffer approach provides a template for AI specific stress testing and capital planning without attempting to call the top of equity valuations.

Source for the Basel III countercyclical buffer range: [92](https://www.federalreserve.gov/publications/annual-report/2010-banking-supervision-regulation.htm).

### 13.2. Regulatory interventions in emerging technology markets

United States money market fund reforms as a template for reducing run risk in high speed funding markets.
- Why this is relevant to AI.
  - AI bubbles can propagate through liquidity and funding channels, including short term funding of intermediaries, prime brokerage, and cash management vehicles that may hold tech linked credit.
  - The money market fund episode is a clear example of regulators addressing structural fragility rather than trying to manage asset prices.
- Key interventions.
  - 2014 reforms introduced tools such as floating net asset value for certain funds and liquidity fees and redemption gates to address run risk.
  - 2023 reforms increased minimum liquidity requirements and required liquidity fees when daily net redemptions exceed 5% of net assets for institutional prime and institutional tax exempt money market funds, while removing the prior gate framework.
- Practical lesson for AI.
  - When a market structure creates first mover advantage and rapid withdrawals, regulators can reduce reflexive dynamics by forcing liquidity buffers and pricing liquidity costs to redeemers.
  - For AI, the analogue is to require liquidity and concentration risk management for funds with heavy exposure to AI infrastructure credit, venture secondaries, or concentrated mega cap tech.

Title: Selected SEC money market fund reform triggers and liquidity requirements

| Reform element | Threshold value | Scope | Adoption date |
|:---|---:|:---|:---|
| Liquidity fee trigger for daily net redemptions percent of net assets | 5.0% | Institutional prime and institutional tax exempt money market funds. | 2023 07 12. |
| Minimum daily liquid assets percent of total assets | 25.0% | Money market funds under amended SEC rules. | 2023 07 12. |
| Minimum weekly liquid assets percent of total assets | 50.0% | Money market funds under amended SEC rules. | 2023 07 12. |

Source: [93](https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2023-129).

Macroprudential reciprocity and calibration example from France as an operational model.
- Why this is relevant to AI.
  - AI investment and supply chains are cross border, so unilateral tightening can be bypassed through offshore financing and regulatory arbitrage.
- Case detail.
  - France publishes countercyclical capital buffer decisions and explicitly references reciprocity for foreign exposures within the European Economic Area framework, illustrating how macroprudential tools can be coordinated across jurisdictions.
- Practical lesson for AI.
  - AI related disclosure and stress testing regimes should be interoperable across major markets, with mutual recognition where possible, to reduce cross border leakage.

Source: [94](https://www.economie.gouv.fr/hcsf-en/counter-cyclical-capital-buffer).

### 13.3. Industry-led self-regulation and best-practice initiatives

Why self regulation matters for AI bubble mitigation.
- Many AI bubble risks are created by private contracting and market norms rather than explicit regulatory gaps.
- Industry can reduce overheating by standardizing disclosures, limiting aggressive revenue recognition narratives, and adopting procurement and financing practices that reduce hidden leverage.

Case pattern from mortgage market data sharing and underwriting norms.
- Although the Hong Kong example is policy led, one of the most transferable mechanisms is the operational practice of sharing borrower mortgage data to prevent concealed leverage across lenders.
- AI analogue.
  - Voluntary, industry supported registries or standardized reporting for large compute commitments and long term capacity reservations could reduce hidden correlated exposures across cloud providers, data center developers, and large AI labs.
  - This is especially relevant where multiple counterparties may be exposed to the same underlying demand assumptions.

Case pattern from liquidity risk management norms in asset management.
- The money market fund reforms show that when liquidity mismatch is a known structural risk, best practice evolves toward higher liquidity buffers, better reporting, and explicit pricing of liquidity costs.
- AI analogue.
  - Asset managers and private credit funds can adopt internal standards that go beyond minimum regulation, including.
    - Concentration limits for single model providers, single cloud providers, and single chip supply chains.
    - Standardized scenario analysis for compute price shocks, power constraints, and demand normalization.
    - Contract transparency norms for take or pay compute and colocation agreements.

Implementation guidance for AI industry best practice.
- Establish a voluntary AI investment disclosure baseline that can later be adopted into regulation.
  - Minimum fields should include AI attributable revenue, AI related capex commitments, compute sourcing concentration, and material model dependencies.
- Adopt financing discipline norms.
  - Discourage circular financing structures and vendor financing that obscures true cost of capital.
- Create shared incident and performance reporting.
  - Similar to operational risk reporting in finance, publish anonymized metrics on model downtime, security incidents, and deployment delays to reduce narrative driven valuation gaps.

Evidence base note.
- Unlike the regulatory case studies above, many industry led initiatives are fragmented and not consistently measured with public quantitative outcomes. For this reason, the most defensible approach is to treat self regulation as a complement to formal disclosure and prudential oversight, and to prioritize initiatives that produce auditable, comparable metrics.

Supporting sources for the underlying mitigation mechanisms.
- HKMA discussion of positive mortgage data sharing and its role in reducing loopholes for concealed leverage: [91](https://www.hkma.gov.hk/eng/news-and-media/insight/2014/07/20140714/).
- SEC rationale for liquidity fees and higher liquidity buffers to reduce run incentives and protect remaining investors from dilution: [93](https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2023-129).

## 14. AI Bubble Contingency Planning

This contingency planning section translates earlier warning indicators and stress testing concepts into operational playbooks that can be activated during an AI market drawdown. The objective is to reduce procyclical amplification, preserve critical AI infrastructure investment where it remains economically justified, and enable rapid reallocation of capital and labor toward productive uses without forcing disorderly fire sales.

### 14.1. Designing early warning systems for AI market overheating

Early warning systems should combine market based signals, balance sheet and funding signals, and real economy capacity signals into a single decision framework with predefined escalation triggers.

Core design principles.
- Use a layered indicator stack that separates valuation heat from leverage and liquidity fragility, because valuation corrections are survivable while leverage and liquidity mismatches can create systemic spillovers.
- Prefer indicators that are hard to game and can be refreshed frequently, including market microstructure, margining activity, and funding conditions.
- Build cross border comparability by anchoring at least one layer to internationally used systemic risk indicators, then add AI specific overlays.

Indicator layers and recommended metrics.
- Macro financial excess credit layer.
  - Track credit to GDP gaps as a standardized early warning indicator for excessive credit conditions that can amplify a tech downturn into a broader credit event.
  - Use BIS credit to GDP gap series for cross country comparability, and supplement with national authority measures where available.
  Source basis for the indicator definition and its early warning role is the BIS credit to GDP gap dataset description and Basel III linkage[95](https://data.bis.org/topics/CREDIT_GAPS) .
- Nonbank leverage and liquidity layer.
  - Monitor leverage and liquidity transformation in nonbank financial intermediation, because AI exposure is often intermediated through funds, structured vehicles, and private credit.
  - Require periodic mapping of AI linked exposures across hedge funds, private credit funds, and open ended funds, including margin sensitivity and redemption terms.
  - Align monitoring with the FSB integrated approach to identifying and monitoring NBFI leverage risks, and incorporate liquidity mismatch monitoring for open ended funds.
  References for leverage amplification and monitoring expectations are the FSB final report on NBFI leverage and the FSB liquidity mismatch recommendations for open ended funds[96](https://www.fsb.org/2025/07/leverage-in-nonbank-financial-intermediation-final-report/) . [97](https://www.fsb.org/2023/12/revised-policy-recommendations-to-address-structural-vulnerabilities-from-liquidity-mismatch-in-open-ended-funds/).
- Private fund and concentrated exposure layer.
  - Use confidential supervisory reporting to detect crowded trades and concentrated AI factor exposures, including large hedge fund exposures, borrowing, counterparty exposure, and portfolio liquidity.
  - In the United States, integrate enhanced Form PF reporting fields and timelines into the early warning dashboard, and treat compliance date changes as operational constraints on data availability.
  The SEC Form PF amendments and compliance date extension provide the reporting basis and timing[98](https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2024-17) . [99](https://www.sec.gov/rules-regulations/2025/06/s7-22-22).
- AI infrastructure capacity and commitment layer.
  - Track forward capex commitments and take or pay contracts for data centers, power procurement, and long lead semiconductors as a proxy for irreversible investment that can become stranded in a downturn.
  - Monitor utilization and pricing proxies where available, including cloud GPU availability, colocation vacancy, and power interconnection queues, to detect overbuild risk.
  - Require scenario based break even analysis for major AI infrastructure projects under demand normalization and compute price decline scenarios.
- Narrative and adoption reality layer.
  - Track divergence between AI revenue realization and AI related capex growth at the firm and sector level.
  - Monitor customer concentration, churn, and contract duration for AI application vendors and model providers.

Escalation logic and governance.
- Establish a three stage escalation ladder.
  - Stage 1 heightened monitoring when valuation heat rises but leverage and liquidity indicators remain stable.
  - Stage 2 preemptive stabilization when leverage, liquidity mismatch, or crowded positioning indicators deteriorate.
  - Stage 3 crisis activation when funding markets tighten, margin calls spike, or redemption pressures emerge.
- Assign ownership.
  - Financial stability authorities own systemic indicators and cross sector exposure mapping.
  - Securities regulators own disclosure integrity, fund liquidity tools readiness, and market conduct surveillance.
  - Energy and infrastructure regulators own power and permitting bottleneck monitoring that can turn a correction into a real economy shock.

Title: Selected systemic early warning reference indicators and latest available global values

| Indicator | Latest value units | Reference period | Primary use case |
|:---|---:|:---|:---|
| NBFI share of total global financial assets | 51.0% | 2024 | Detect nonbank amplification channels for AI linked risk assets. |
| NBFI total global financial assets | 256,800,000,000,000.00 USD | 2024 | Size systemic exposure surface area for forced selling risk. |
| Narrow measure NBFI financial assets | 76,300,000,000,000.00 USD | 2024 | Focus monitoring on bank like credit intermediation risks. |


![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Source: Financial Stability Board Global Monitoring Report on Nonbank Financial Intermediation and related 2025 release summarizing 2024 results[75](https://www.fsb.org/2025/12/fsb-reports-continued-growth-in-nonbank-financial-intermediation-in-2024-to-258-trillion/) .

Operational outputs.
- A weekly AI market stability dashboard for policymakers and a monthly supervisory pack for regulated entities.
- A quarterly cross border tabletop exercise that tests data sharing, margin call response, and fund liquidity tool activation.
- A public communication template that explains what is being monitored and why, without signaling a target level for AI valuations.

### 14.2. Coordinated crisis response across regulators and industry stakeholders

A coordinated response should aim to prevent a valuation correction from becoming a funding and liquidity crisis, while maintaining market integrity and avoiding ad hoc interventions that create moral hazard.

Crisis coordination architecture.
- Establish an AI market stability coordination group with standing membership from.
  - Central banks and macroprudential authorities.
  - Securities regulators and market supervisors.
  - Banking supervisors and resolution authorities.
  - Competition and data protection authorities where platform concentration and data access constraints can worsen stress.
  - Energy and critical infrastructure regulators due to the power intensive nature of AI infrastructure.
- Define a single incident commander model for information flow, with clear triggers for convening and for elevating to ministerial level.

Shared situational awareness and data sharing.
- Activate a common exposure map within 72 hours of a Stage 3 trigger.
  - Identify concentrated AI factor exposures across funds, banks, and insurers.
  - Identify leverage providers and key counterparties, including prime brokers and derivatives clearing members.
- Use existing confidential reporting channels where possible.
  - In the United States, incorporate Form PF data sharing arrangements between the SEC and CFTC and the systemic risk monitoring objective described by the SEC, recognizing that compliance timing affects data freshness[98](https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2024-17) . [99](https://www.sec.gov/rules-regulations/2025/06/s7-22-22).
- Coordinate internationally through the Financial Stability Board for nonbank leverage and liquidity vulnerabilities, using the FSB monitoring framework and leverage recommendations as a common language for action[76](https://www.fsb.org/2025/07/fsb-publishes-recommendations-to-address-financial-stability-risks-created-by-leverage-in-nonbank-financial-intermediation/) .

Market functioning and liquidity measures.
- Prioritize tools that support market functioning rather than asset prices.
  - Ensure operational readiness for margin and collateral management, including intraday liquidity planning at key intermediaries.
  - Encourage use of fund liquidity management tools where permitted, consistent with the FSB recommendations to reduce structural liquidity mismatch and remove barriers to tool use[97](https://www.fsb.org/2023/12/revised-policy-recommendations-to-address-structural-vulnerabilities-from-liquidity-mismatch-in-open-ended-funds/) .
- Coordinate supervisory expectations for leverage providers.
  - Tighten counterparty risk management and margin practices in a harmonized way to avoid cliff effects and regulatory arbitrage, consistent with the FSB emphasis on monitoring and containing NBFI leverage risks and interlinkages with leverage providers[96](https://www.fsb.org/2025/07/leverage-in-nonbank-financial-intermediation-final-report/) .

Industry stakeholder actions.
- Cloud providers, data center operators, and chip suppliers.
  - Provide standardized disclosures on capacity utilization, committed capex, and cancellation terms to reduce uncertainty driven selling.
  - Coordinate on continuity planning for critical AI services used in healthcare, finance, and government.
- Institutional investors and asset managers.
  - Pre commit to liquidity management playbooks, including redemption gates where legally available, swing pricing where applicable, and transparent communication to investors.
  - Conduct rapid portfolio concentration reviews focused on AI factor crowding and correlated exposures.
- Rating agencies and auditors.
  - Accelerate impairment and going concern assessments for AI infrastructure projects with high leverage and weak contracted cash flows.

Communications strategy.
- Use a single cross agency message that.
  - Distinguishes valuation repricing from systemic stress.
  - Explains the operational steps being taken to maintain market functioning.
  - Provides a timeline for next updates to reduce rumor driven volatility.

Cross border coordination priorities.
- Align definitions of AI linked exposures for supervisory reporting to reduce blind spots.
- Share stress scenarios and results templates so that multinational firms can report consistently.
- Coordinate enforcement against AI washing and misleading risk disclosures to prevent fraud spikes during downturns.

### 14.3. Strategies for controlled market repricing and orderly correction

Orderly correction strategies should accept that repricing is necessary when expectations reset, while preventing forced selling spirals, disorderly defaults, and abrupt cancellation of economically viable long horizon projects.

Principles for controlled repricing.
- Separate solvency problems from liquidity problems.
  - Liquidity support should be conditional and time bound, aimed at market functioning.
  - Insolvent projects should be restructured quickly to avoid zombie capex.
- Reduce cliff effects.
  - Avoid sudden regulatory changes that force immediate deleveraging or mass reclassification of assets.
  - Use phased implementation and supervisory guidance to smooth adjustment.

Tools to dampen procyclicality in funds and nonbanks.
- Activate liquidity management tools in open ended funds where permitted.
  - Encourage swing pricing, redemption fees, notice periods, and temporary gates consistent with the FSB recommendation that authorities ensure a broad set of liquidity management tools is available and usable[97](https://www.fsb.org/2023/12/revised-policy-recommendations-to-address-structural-vulnerabilities-from-liquidity-mismatch-in-open-ended-funds/) .
- Coordinate leverage containment.
  - Apply consistent supervisory expectations for margining and counterparty risk management to avoid destabilizing margin spirals, aligned with the FSB leverage recommendations[96](https://www.fsb.org/2025/07/leverage-in-nonbank-financial-intermediation-final-report/) .

Orderly repricing in private markets.
- Establish restructuring pathways for AI infrastructure and venture backed firms.
  - Standardize standstill agreements and creditor coordination frameworks for data center and compute financing structures.
  - Encourage debt for equity conversions where projects have viable long term contracted demand.
- Improve price discovery.
  - Promote secondary market mechanisms for private AI assets with standardized disclosure packs to reduce valuation opacity.
  - Use enhanced private fund reporting to identify concentrated exposures and reduce surprise liquidations, consistent with the SEC objective of improving systemic risk monitoring through Form PF[98](https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2024-17) .

Orderly correction in public markets.
- Focus on market integrity and transparency.
  - Intensify surveillance for manipulation, misleading AI claims, and disorderly trading conditions.
  - Require rapid clarification of material AI revenue and capex disclosures when volatility spikes.
- Use existing market wide circuit breakers and volatility controls as designed, avoiding bespoke AI specific trading halts that could signal panic.

Real economy stabilization for AI adjacent sectors.
- Maintain continuity of critical AI services.
  - Identify systemically important AI service dependencies in finance and government and require continuity plans.
- Protect productive investment.
  - Prioritize permitting and grid interconnection clarity for projects with strong contracted cash flows, while pausing or renegotiating speculative builds.
- Labor market contingency.
  - Coordinate rapid reemployment and reskilling programs for displaced tech workers to reduce second round demand shocks.

Post correction opportunity framework.
- Establish a recovery capital channel.
  - Create criteria for deploying patient capital into distressed but viable AI infrastructure and application firms, emphasizing unit economics, governance, and compliance readiness.
- Require after action reviews.
  - Within 90 days of stabilization, publish a cross agency lessons learned report focused on data gaps, leverage channels, and disclosure failures.

Title: Selected supervisory data readiness milestones relevant to crisis monitoring in the United States

| Item | Date | Status | Relevance to AI downturn monitoring |
|:---|---:|:---|:---|
| SEC adoption of Form PF amendments | 2024-02-08 | Adopted | Expands private fund exposure, leverage, and liquidity reporting for systemic risk monitoring. |
| Form PF amendments compliance date extension | 2026-10-01 | Scheduled | Delays availability of enhanced standardized data during 2025 and early 2026 stress episodes. |

Source: SEC press release on Form PF amendments and SEC final rule extending the compliance date[98](https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2024-17) . [99](https://www.sec.gov/rules-regulations/2025/06/s7-22-22).

## 15. Strategic Recommendations for Different Stakeholders

This section translates the report’s bubble risk indicators, valuation and financing dynamics, and policy gap analysis into stakeholder specific actions that reduce procyclicality, improve price discovery, and preserve innovation capacity during both boom and correction phases. Recommendations prioritize measurable controls, governance, and disclosure practices that can be implemented without requiring stakeholders to forecast the exact timing of a market turning point.

### 15.1. Tailored guidance for institutional investors navigating AI risk

Portfolio construction and exposure controls.
- Treat AI exposure as a factor cluster rather than a sector label, separating exposure to compute capex cycles, hyperscaler concentration, semiconductor supply chains, and application layer adoption risk.
- Set explicit concentration limits for single name and single platform dependencies, including indirect exposure through index products, private funds, and structured notes.
- Use a barbell approach that pairs high duration AI growth exposure with cash flow resilient enablers such as power, grid equipment, and data center real assets, while stress testing correlation spikes during risk off regimes.

Underwriting discipline for private markets.
- Require evidence of repeatable unit economics under realistic inference costs, including sensitivity to model price compression and cloud margin pressure.
- Avoid valuation anchoring to headline mega rounds by re pricing using downside scenarios for compute availability, customer churn, and regulatory delays.
- Prefer financing structures that reduce reflexivity, including staged capital with milestone based tranches, capped liquidation preferences, and covenants tied to cash burn and gross margin.

Public equity and credit risk management.
- Separate AI narrative premium from earnings durability by tracking revenue concentration, capex intensity, and the share of growth attributable to AI specific products versus broader cloud or advertising cycles.
- For credit and infrastructure debt, require covenants that address utilization risk, power price volatility, and counterparty concentration, especially where revenues depend on a small set of frontier model customers.
- Implement liquidity planning for private AI holdings, including secondary market readiness and pre negotiated continuation vehicle terms.

Operational due diligence and governance.
- Require board level AI risk oversight for portfolio companies, including model risk management, cybersecurity, and compliance readiness for major jurisdictions.
- Demand auditable reporting on AI claims to reduce AI washing risk, including third party assurance where feasible.
- For investors using AI internally, align with supervisory expectations on model risk management and data governance, and document human oversight for material investment decisions.

Early warning dashboard for investment committees.
- Track private AI funding acceleration and mega round dependence as a proxy for late cycle behavior.
- Monitor market concentration and capex escalation among the largest AI beneficiaries as a systemic signal.

Title: Selected quantitative indicators relevant to AI bubble risk monitoring.

| Indicator | Value | Period | Why it matters |
|:---|---:|:---:|:---|
| Global generative AI VC funding | 56,000,000,000.00 USD | 2024 | Rapid funding growth can amplify narrative driven valuations and reduce underwriting discipline. |
| Global generative AI VC deals | 885.00 deals | 2024 | Deal count helps distinguish broad based adoption from a small number of mega rounds. |
| Private AI company funding | 105,700,000,000.00 USD | 2024 | High aggregate funding can increase correlated exposure across portfolios and supply chains. |
| Private AI company funding | 116,100,000,000.00 USD | 2025 H1 | Funding pace exceeding the prior year can signal late cycle dynamics and rising correction risk. |


![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Source: PitchBook data reported by TechCrunch for generative AI funding and deals in 2024 [100](https://techcrunch.com/2025/01/03/generative-ai-funding-reached-new-heights-in-2024/). CB Insights State of AI Q2 2025 for global private AI funding totals for 2024 and 2025 H1 [101](https://www.cbinsights.com/research/report/ai-trends-q2-2025/).

Implementation checklist for institutional investors.
- Define AI exposure taxonomy across public equity, private equity, venture, infrastructure, and credit holdings.
- Adopt a minimum disclosure request list for AI investees covering revenue composition, compute commitments, and platform dependencies.
- Run quarterly stress tests using scenarios that include compute price shocks, power constraints, demand normalization, and model commoditization.
- Pre commit rebalancing rules that reduce procyclicality, including trimming rules for extreme multiple expansion and adding rules for post correction quality assets.

Key watch outs for 2025 and 2026 positioning.
- Mega round driven funding can mask weakening breadth in early stage formation and exits, increasing liquidity risk for late stage private holdings [102](https://fortune.com/2025/04/24/in-q1-2025-ai-commanded-71-of-total-vc-deal-value-according-to-pitchbook/).
- Large private valuations can embed governance and restructuring contingencies that create cliff risk for investors, as illustrated by OpenAI’s 40,000,000,000.00 USD round and restructuring condition timelines [103](https://www.cnbc.com/2025/03/31/openai-closes-40-billion-in-funding-the-largest-private-fundraise-in-history-softbank-chatgpt.html).

### 15.2. Actionable policy recommendations for regulators and governments

Improve market transparency and comparability.
- Mandate standardized AI exposure disclosures for public companies and large private issuers accessing public like markets, including AI attributable revenue, capex commitments, compute supply dependencies, and material model licensing arrangements.
- Require substantiation standards for AI related marketing claims to reduce AI washing, using audit ready definitions and enforcement aligned with investor protection mandates.
- Promote machine readable reporting for AI risk and capex commitments to enable supervisory analytics and cross border comparability.

Target amplification channels rather than asset prices.
- Tighten supervision of leverage and maturity mismatch in nonbank financial intermediation that funds AI linked equities, private credit, and data center infrastructure.
- Apply macroprudential tools where credit growth and correlated exposures are building, using established countercyclical frameworks rather than AI specific price targeting.

Title: Macroprudential tool template applicable to AI linked credit cycles.

| Tool | Objective | Mechanism | Reference basis |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Countercyclical capital buffer | Increase resilience during credit booms. | Raises bank capital requirements in periods of excess credit growth and can be released in downturns. | Basel Committee CCyB framework [104](https://www.bis.org/bcbs/ccyb/). |
| Supervisory stress testing | Identify vulnerabilities before repricing events. | Standardized scenarios applied to banks and systemically important intermediaries with AI linked exposures. | Basel Committee guidance on CCyB and macro financial environment linkages [104](https://www.bis.org/bcbs/ccyb/). |

Source: Basel Committee on Banking Supervision CCyB overview and related guidance [104](https://www.bis.org/bcbs/ccyb/).

Align AI governance with financial stability and competition policy.
- Coordinate financial regulators, competition authorities, and digital regulators to monitor concentration in cloud and model markets, since platform dependency can transmit shocks across the economy.
- Use procurement and public sector AI adoption to set standards for auditability, security, and interoperability, reducing lock in and improving market discipline.

Support sustainable infrastructure build out.
- Accelerate permitting and grid interconnection reforms for data centers and clean power where bottlenecks create boom bust capex cycles.
- Require resilience planning for critical AI infrastructure, including cybersecurity baselines and incident reporting, to reduce tail risk that can trigger abrupt repricing.

Cross border coordination.
- Pursue mutual recognition of AI related disclosure and assurance standards to reduce regulatory arbitrage and improve comparability for global investors.
- Establish shared supervisory typologies for AI linked exposures across banking, insurance, and asset management, enabling faster coordinated responses during stress.

Contingency planning for orderly corrections.
- Prepare liquidity backstops and resolution playbooks for systemically important market utilities and intermediaries that could be stressed by a rapid repricing.
- Communicate clearly that policy aims to preserve market functioning and credit transmission, not to guarantee AI asset valuations, to reduce moral hazard.
