Policy sequencing guidance.
- Phase in disclosure and stress testing requirements first, then tighten leverage and underwriting standards where evidence shows amplification, to avoid sudden regulatory shocks that can accelerate a correction.

### 15.3. Responsibilities and strategic roles for AI industry leaders

Capital discipline and truthful signaling.
- Publish credible capex roadmaps that link compute expansion to contracted demand and utilization targets, reducing the risk of overbuild dynamics.
- Avoid revenue recognition practices or customer incentive structures that inflate near term growth at the expense of durable margins.
- Provide investors with clear separation between research milestones and commercial readiness, including limitations and deployment constraints.

Disclosure and assurance leadership.
- Voluntarily adopt standardized AI metrics ahead of regulation, including AI attributable revenue, inference cost per unit, and dependency on third party models or cloud providers.
- Commission independent assurance for material AI claims and safety controls, improving trust and reducing information asymmetry.

Risk management and safety as financial stability inputs.
- Treat cybersecurity, model misuse, and reliability failures as balance sheet risks, with board level oversight and tested incident response plans.
- Implement robust evaluation and monitoring for model updates, including rollback procedures, to reduce operational shocks that can trigger market repricing.

Competition and ecosystem health.
- Support interoperability and portability where feasible, including standard APIs and data export mechanisms, to reduce systemic lock in and concentration fragility.
- Use responsible partnership structures with startups that avoid hidden leverage and circular financing, including transparent compute credits and revenue share terms.

Labor and capability resilience.
- Invest in workforce transition programs and internal reskilling to reduce boom bust hiring cycles and preserve execution capacity during downturns.
- Maintain R and D continuity plans that protect core research during funding contractions, reducing the probability that a market correction becomes an innovation winter.

Public engagement and policy collaboration.
- Participate in regulatory sandboxes and standards bodies to help define auditable, implementable rules for AI disclosures and safety practices.
- Share non sensitive incident learnings and best practices through industry consortia to reduce correlated operational failures.

Contingency planning commitments.
- Maintain liquidity buffers and diversified funding sources to withstand a sudden tightening in capital markets.
- Pre define cost reduction levers that preserve customer support, security, and compliance functions, since cutting these areas can amplify reputational and regulatory shocks during a downturn.

## 16. Citations and References

This section consolidates the citation approach, primary datasets, and recommended sources used to support the report’s analysis of AI bubble risk, valuation dynamics, investment trends, and policy and investor mitigation strategies. References prioritize primary sources, official statistics, and widely used market datasets current as of January 26, 2026.

### 16.1. Citation Standards

Preferred standard.
- Use APA 7th edition for all in text citations and the reference list.

In text citation rules.
- Use author or organization and year for narrative or parenthetical citations.
- For institutional reports with frequent reuse, define an abbreviation on first use and apply consistently.
- For web pages that update over time, include the publication date shown on the page. If no date is available, use n.d. and include an accessed date.

Reference list rules.
- Use sentence case for report and web page titles.
- Include publisher for reports when different from the authoring organization.
- Include DOI when available. Otherwise include a stable URL.
- For datasets and dashboards, cite the dataset name, version or release date, publisher, and URL.

Evidence hierarchy used in this report.
- Tier 1.
  - Official statistics and regulators.
  - Central bank and international financial institutions.
  - Audited company filings.
- Tier 2.
  - Reputable market data providers and index providers.
  - Peer reviewed research and top tier working papers.
- Tier 3.
  - High quality journalism used only to corroborate market events when primary data is not accessible.

Reference list entries used in this report.
- International Monetary Fund. (2025, April 22). Global financial stability report April 2025 enhancing resilience amid uncertainty. International Monetary Fund. https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/GFSR/Issues/2025/04/22/global-financial-stability-report-april-2025.
- Organisation for Economic Co operation and Development. (2025, June 3). Global economic outlook shifts as trade policy uncertainty weakens growth. OECD. https://read.oecd-ilibrary.org/en/about/news/press-releases/2025/06/global-economic-outlook-shifts-as-trade-policy-uncertainty-weakens-growth.html.
- Stanford Institute for Human Centered Artificial Intelligence. (2025). The 2025 AI index report. Stanford University. https://hai.stanford.edu/ai-index/2025-ai-index-report?u=.
- Stanford Institute for Human Centered Artificial Intelligence. (2024). The 2024 AI index report. Stanford University. https://hai.stanford.edu/ai-index/2024-ai-index-report.
- Artificial Intelligence Act. (2024). Implementation timeline. https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/implementation-timeline/.
- TechCrunch. (2025, January 3). Generative AI funding reached new heights in 2024. https://techcrunch.com/2025/01/03/generative-ai-funding-reached-new-heights-in-2024/.

### 16.2. Data Sources

Core market and investment datasets used.
- AI private investment and adoption.
  - Stanford HAI AI Index 2025 for global and regional private AI investment and adoption indicators. https://hai.stanford.edu/ai-index/2025-ai-index-report?u=.
- Venture capital and deal activity.
  - PitchBook figures as reported by TechCrunch for generative AI venture funding totals and deal counts. https://techcrunch.com/2025/01/03/generative-ai-funding-reached-new-heights-in-2024/.
- Macro financial stability and valuation vulnerability framing.
  - IMF Global Financial Stability Report April 2025 for global valuation vulnerability framing and financial stability risk channels. https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/GFSR/Issues/2025/04/22/global-financial-stability-report-april-2025.
- Macro baseline and downside context.
  - OECD Economic Outlook press release June 2025 for global growth projections and financial conditions context. https://read.oecd-ilibrary.org/en/about/news/press-releases/2025/06/global-economic-outlook-shifts-as-trade-policy-uncertainty-weakens-growth.html.
- Regulatory timelines affecting deployment costs and compliance risk.
  - EU AI Act implementation timeline for entry into force and phased applicability dates. https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/implementation-timeline/.

Title: Selected quantitative indicators referenced in the report.

| Indicator | Value (units) | Period (units) | Source |
|:---|---:|:---|:---|
| Global generative AI VC funding | 56,000,000,000.00 USD | 2024.00 year | https://techcrunch.com/2025/01/03/generative-ai-funding-reached-new-heights-in-2024/. |
| Global generative AI VC deals | 885.00 deals | 2024.00 year | https://techcrunch.com/2025/01/03/generative-ai-funding-reached-new-heights-in-2024/. |
| Global generative AI VC funding | 29,100,000,000.00 USD | 2023.00 year | https://techcrunch.com/2025/01/03/generative-ai-funding-reached-new-heights-in-2024/. |
| U.S. private AI investment | 109,100,000,000.00 USD | 2024.00 year | https://hai.stanford.edu/ai-index/2025-ai-index-report?u=. |


![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Source: Stanford HAI AI Index 2025 and PitchBook figures as reported by TechCrunch. https://hai.stanford.edu/ai-index/2025-ai-index-report?u=. https://techcrunch.com/2025/01/03/generative-ai-funding-reached-new-heights-in-2024/.

Data reliability and limitations.
- Private market valuation and funding data can be revised as late filings and deal amendments are incorporated. Use the latest release of the underlying provider when replicating results.
- Journalism sourced market figures are used only when the underlying dataset is not directly accessible in the report workflow. Where possible, replace with direct PitchBook, CB Insights, Preqin, or Refinitiv extracts in a production version.
- Regulatory timelines should be verified against official publications when used for compliance planning. The cited EU AI Act timeline is a secondary consolidation and should be cross checked with the Official Journal for legal certainty.

### 16.3. Further Reading

Financial stability and bubble diagnostics.
- International Monetary Fund. Global financial stability report series. https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/GFSR.
- Bank for International Settlements. BIS working papers and Quarterly Review on financial cycles and market structure. https://www.bis.org.
- Financial Stability Board. Publications on non bank financial intermediation and systemic risk monitoring. https://www.fsb.org.

AI investment measurement and market structure.
- Stanford HAI. AI Index report series. https://hai.stanford.edu/ai-index.
- OECD. AI policy observatory and AI measurement work. https://oecd.ai.

Regulation and governance.
- European Union. EU AI Act implementation resources and timeline consolidation. https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/implementation-timeline/.

Historical tech bubbles and valuation context.
- Academic and policy literature on the dot com episode and technology diffusion, including central bank and academic working papers accessible via BIS and IMF portals. https://www.bis.org. https://www.imf.org.

Investor due diligence and risk management.
- CFA Institute research on technology investing, valuation under uncertainty, and risk governance. https://www.cfainstitute.org.
- IOSCO publications on retail investor protection, market integrity, and disclosure practices. https://www.iosco.org.
